data.title: CoinGecko: RWA Industry Report 2026
data.description:
Source: CoinGecko

Since the resurgence of RWA in 2024, increased regulatory transparency has allowed major traditional financial institutions to test the waters. As early experiments have evolved into best practices and operational guidelines, the pace of tokenization has accelerated significantly.

2025 became a watershed year for the RWA industry. Competition across the entire RWA and tokenization arena has intensified for both native crypto institutions and traditional financial participants; asset issuers have begun to build differentiated advantages from three dimensions: compliance qualifications, asset coverage, and distribution reach. Centralized exchanges have also led the popularization of the sector with their exclusive traditional financial strategies, further lowering the entry barrier for trading these assets. This report reviews the overall development of the RWA industry from 2025 to the first quarter of 2026.

Seven Highlights from CoinGecko's 2026 RWA Report

  1. Since 2025, the size of tokenized RWA has more than tripled, reaching $19.3 billion by the end of Q1 2026.

  2. Tokenized commodity trading volume increased from $1.4 billion to $5.5 billion, primarily driven by gold-backed tokens XAUT and PAXG.

  3. In Q1 2026, spot trading volume of tokenized gold reached $90.7 billion, surpassing the $84.6 billion trading volume for the entire year of 2025.

  4. Since its launch in mid-2025, the market capitalization of tokenized stocks has reached $500 million, with technology stocks leading the way.

  5. In the first quarter of 2026, the total spot trading volume of tokenized stocks reached $15.1 billion, surpassing the $14.8 billion of the second half of 2025. Tokenized ETFs reached $300 million in size, demonstrating broad growth and a considerable long-tail effect. In the first quarter of 2026, the total value of RWA (Risk-Weighted Assets) jumped to $524.8 billion, far exceeding the $313 billion for the entire year of 2025. 1. Since 2025, the size of tokenized RWA has more than tripled, reaching $19.3 billion by the end of the first quarter of 2026. The total market capitalization of tokenized RWA has grown by 256.7% in 15 months, from $5.42 billion at the beginning of 2025 to $19.32 billion as of March 31, 2026. While tokenized RWA's market capitalization represents only 6.4% of the stablecoin market capitalization, this percentage is higher than the 2.7% at the beginning of 2025, indicating that tokenized RWA has outpaced stablecoins in growth over the past year. Tokenized Treasury bonds remain the largest asset class, with their market capitalization increasing by $9 billion (+225.5%) during this period, accounting for more than half of the sector's market capitalization growth. Notably, their growth momentum accelerated slightly after their market capitalization first surpassed the $10 billion mark on February 11, 2026. Despite this, the market share of tokenized Treasury bonds has slightly decreased from 73.7% to 67.2% as other asset classes have become more tokenized and gained greater market acceptance. As of the end of Q1 2026, tokenized commodities accounted for 28.7% of the sector, tokenized equities for 2.5%, and tokenized ETFs for 1.5%. 2. Tokenized commodity prices surged 289% to $5.5 billion, primarily driven by gold-backed tokens XAUT and PAXG.

    During the same period, the market capitalization of tokenized goods increased from $1.43 billion to $5.55 billion, an increase of $4.12 billion (+289.1%). Previously, this asset class first broke the $5 billion mark on January 19, 2026, then reached a peak of $6.69 billion on February 5, before falling back 20.6% and finally closing at the end of the quarter.

    However, the tokenized commodities market remains largely dominated by gold-backed tokens—particularly Tether's XAUT and Paxos' PAXG, which contributed $1.87 billion and $1.8 billion respectively, accounting for 89.1% of market expansion. This aligns with the continued upward trend in spot gold prices over the past year. PAXG saw the largest increase in market share, growing from 36.8% to 41.8% of the tokenized commodities market, reaching a market capitalization of $2.32 billion. XAUT continues to lead with a market capitalization of $2.52 billion, though its market share, after reaching 54.7% at the end of October 2025, has since fallen back to 45.5%. Despite the increase in absolute market capitalization, smaller precious metal tokens have relatively shrunk. Kinesis's KAG is the largest silver-backed token by market capitalization, more than doubling to $350 million, but its market share declined from 8.0% to 4.8%. Similarly, KAU's market capitalization increased by $230 million to $270 million, but its market share also declined from 8.5% to 6.3%. On the other hand, Matrixdock's XAUM grew 11 times to $700 million, with its market share rising from 0.4% to 1.3%, ranking among the top five. In the first quarter of 2026, spot trading volume of tokenized gold reached $90.7 billion, exceeding the $84.6 billion trading volume for the entire year of 2025.

    In the first quarter of 2026 alone, the total spot trading volume of tokenized gold reached $90.7 billion, exceeding the $84.64 billion for the entire year of 2025. This reflects the surge in demand for tokenized gold driven by cryptocurrency participants seeking to invest in this high-performing asset, as well as the increasing convenience of exchange trading channels. Notably, centralized exchanges (CEXs) account for the vast majority of spot trading in tokenized assets.

    However, to date, the spot trading volume of tokenized gold has fluctuated significantly each month, reflecting its sensitivity to market conditions.

    For example, in October 2025, as gold prices hit record highs, spot trading volume surged to $21.38 billion, more than three times the $6.73 billion trading volume of the previous month, before falling back to $14.07 billion the following month. Similar to the market capitalization of tokenized commodities, PAXG and XAUT dominate tokenized gold spot trading. During this period, PAXG's monthly trading volume share ranged from 34.2% to 82.5%, while XAUT's monthly trading volume share ranged from 14.8% to 64.6%. Over the past fifteen months, PAXG and XAUT's average monthly spot trading volume was $5.72 billion and $5.32 billion, respectively, while their average monthly total trading volume was $11.69 billion. Meanwhile, KAG's average monthly trading volume is $570 million, Tether's full-chain deployment XAUT0's trading volume is $100 million, while XAUM's trading volume is only $7 million. 4. Since its launch in mid-2025, the market capitalization of tokenized stocks has reached $500 million, with technology stocks leading the way.

    Tokenized stocks began launching in the middle of last year, and their market capitalization grew from $2.09 million on June 30, 2025 to $486.69 million on March 31, 2026. Backed Finance's xStocks saw its first tokens listed by the end of July 2025, bringing the asset class's market capitalization to $28.59 million. Tokenized stocks from Tesla, Circle, Nvidia, and Alphabet also began trading on-chain.

    ... By the end of September 2025, Ondo's tokenized stock offering further boosted its market capitalization, tripling it to $111.21 million. Circle has become the largest tokenized stock by market capitalization to date, reaching $171.39 million, representing 35.2% of the asset class. This includes $130.3 million for its Ondo tokenized product CRCLON and $41.08 million for its xStock tokenized product CRCLX. Tesla initially launched as the largest tokenized stock, peaking at $73.8 million in late January 2026, but subsequently fell back to $61.7 million. Correspondingly, Tesla's market share grew from an initial 17.2% to a dominant 32.2% in August 2025, before declining again to 12.7%. In comparison, Tesla's xStock token TSLAX has a larger market capitalization of $47.77 million, while its Ondo token TSLON has a market capitalization of $13.93 million. Other leading tokenized stocks by market capitalization include Nvidia ($42.59 million, 8.8% share), Alphabet ($36.91 million, 7.6% share), and MicroStrategy ($26.15 million, 5.4% share). 5. In the first quarter of 2026, the total spot trading volume of tokenized stocks reached $15.1 billion, surpassing the $14.8 billion of the second half of 2025. When tokenized stocks launched in July 2025, monthly spot trading volume was only $2.33 billion. Although it declined in the following two months, tokenized stocks' spot trading volume has since exceeded $4 billion for four consecutive months. Similar to tokenized gold, total spot trading volume for tokenized stocks reached $15.12 billion in the first quarter of 2026, surpassing the $14.84 billion of the last two quarters of 2025. Tesla, Circle, and Nvidia contribute the most to the trading volume of tokenized stocks, averaging $540 million, $450 million, and $440 million per month, respectively, to date. Despite integration with multiple centralized exchanges (CEXs), the trading activity of the top 5 tokenized stocks remains negligible compared to their real-world counterparts, representing less than 1% of the total trading volume in the TradFi stock market. 6. Tokenized ETFs have reached $300 million in size, with a broad growth base and a considerable long-tail effect. The market capitalization of tokenized ETFs grew from $620,000 on July 1, 2025, when the products were still in the launch phase, to $297.5 million by the end of the first quarter of 2026. This makes the market capitalization of tokenized ETFs slightly more than half that of tokenized stocks. Notably, the launch of Ondo's tokenized ETF increased the market capitalization of this asset class from $950,000 on September 1, 2025, to $197.66 million by the end of the month. Compared to tokenized stocks, the growth of tokenized ETFs is more widespread, with no clear leader yet emerging. Ondo's SPDR S&P 500 ETF remains the largest tokenized ETF by market capitalization, at $32.45 million, representing a 10.9% market share. iShares Silver Trust follows closely with a market capitalization of $31.75 million and a 10.7% market share. Other large tokenized ETFs have market shares ranging from 4.5% to 8.6%. Meanwhile, a significant number of smaller tokenized ETFs collectively account for 27.8% of the market share as of March 31, 2026. 7. In the first quarter of 2026, total risk-weighted assets (RWA) jumped to $524.8 billion, far exceeding the $313 billion for the entire year of 2025.

    Perpetual contracts have become another tool for trading RWA or TradeFi assets, and have achieved significant growth in tandem with tokenization. In the first quarter of 2026 alone, the total trading volume of RWA perpetual contracts reached $524.79 billion, on track to more than double the $313.02 billion for the entire year of 2025. This marks the fourth consecutive quarter of growth in RWA perpetual contract trading volume, from $29.74 billion in the first quarter of 2025 to $67.41 billion in the second quarter, $77 billion in the third quarter, and $138.87 billion in the fourth quarter.

    ... Commodities initially accounted for the majority of all RWA perpetual contract trading until July 2025. However, in the past six months, their monthly trading volume share has fluctuated between 69.7% and 95.3%. Meanwhile, perpetual contracts in other asset classes have also gained market acceptance: the market share of equity perpetual contracts rose from 0.4% in August 2025 to 6.0% in March 2026; the market share of ETF perpetual contracts increased from 2.8% in October 2025 to 5.3% in March 2026, although most of this growth came from SPY trading volume. Furthermore, Hyperliquid's HIP-3 platform's share of monthly RWA trading volume increased from 2.8% at its launch in October 2025 to 28.6% in March 2026. Notably, HIP-3 trading volume also surged from $12.65 billion in Q4 2025 to $130.87 billion in Q1 2026. RWA perpetual bonds' daily open interest jumped from $140 million on January 1, 2025 to $6.68 billion on March 31, 2026. In Q1 2026, RWA perpetual bonds averaged $4.82 billion in daily open interest, more than five times the average of $850 million per day last year.

data.title: OKX's payment protocol has enabled the agent economy to take root.
data.description:
Web3's initial promise was to allow value to flow as freely as information. However, for over a decade, its primary use cases have revolved around price discovery and asset trading; it has only addressed the question of "how much is this token worth," without truly answering "what can it be used for?" Meanwhile, AI has undergone a crucial leap in the past two years: from "being able to speak" to "being able to do things." Tool calls, Agent frameworks, and the MCP protocol have emerged, enabling AI to autonomously manage wallets and initiate on-chain transactions. But a new problem has arisen—when Agents become new "on-chain users" and need to collaborate with other Agent users, what "currency" do they use for transactions? What mechanism guarantees contract fulfillment? Who arbitrates when problems arise? These two threads converge on the same answer today: OKX Onchain OS released its Agent Payments Protocol (hereinafter referred to as the APP) yesterday—an open payment standard designed specifically for AI Agent business activities, and one of the most complete Agent economic infrastructures to date. What problem does the APP protocol solve? Does the term "protocol" sound a bit abstract? Let's use a concrete scenario to intuitively understand the design of the OKX APP. Recently, a certain Web3 concept track has suddenly become very popular, and you want to figure out which projects to follow as soon as possible. You've assigned this task to your transaction AI Agent (let's call it Beta). After breaking down the task, Beta discovered that to provide reliable advice, it needed three types of data: on-chain fund flows, the funding background of each project, and a security assessment of the contract code. Therefore, it released three sub-tasks on-chain, with a total budget of 50 USDG and a delivery time of 24 hours. At this point, the **APP's** guarantee mechanism was activated—50 USDG was automatically locked on-chain, ensuring the security of your payment funds. The three data agents who accepted the order also confirmed the authenticity of the payment, allowing you to start working with confidence. Five professional agents simultaneously accepted the bid, and Beta partnered with the three who first reached an agreement: the on-chain data agent was responsible for tracking fund inflows and outflows and changes in whale holdings, at a fee of 15 USDG; the funding data agent was responsible for crawling the funding rounds, investors, and valuations of each project, at a fee of 20 USDG; and the contract audit agent was responsible for scanning for code vulnerabilities and risk levels, at a fee of 15 USDG. **APP's** negotiation mechanism comes into play:** All three parties reach agreements with Beta on-chain, contracts are automatically confirmed, and each party begins working independently without any human intermediaries. 24 hours later, three sub-reports are submitted. **APP's** verification and settlement mechanism is triggered:** Beta automatically checks according to preset standards: Does the on-chain data cover all projects? Is the financing information traceable? Does the contract audit provide a clear risk rating? If all three reports pass, the three secured funds are automatically released, and the payment is credited instantly. **If any report is missing data or does not meet requirements, the funds remain locked, and **APP's** built-in dispute resolution process is automatically initiated—instead of relying solely on manual coordination or resulting in payment without receiving satisfactory deliverables. Ultimately, Beta integrates the three reports into a comprehensive analysis covering funding, fundamentals, and security, and sends it directly to you. After reviewing it, you can formulate your trading strategy based on this analysis. The entire process involves no human intervention, no invoices, no manual transfers, no need to chase tasks, and no unresolved disputes. How does the APP protocol support Agent commercialization? Previously, the payment capabilities of AI Agents were at a very rudimentary stage. Most existing protocols only addressed the basic issue of "payment," corresponding to the basic logic of the HTTP-402 protocol: simple and direct, but extremely limited. The more fundamental problem is that these solutions are designed for services built for humans, not for autonomous commercial transactions between Agents. What the APP aims to fill is precisely this gap: it's not just about enabling Agents to make payments, but about enabling Agents to confidently conduct business. And you, you only need to see the results and make decisions.

Upgrade of Business Paradigm:Compared to existing solutions, the difference in the APP lies not in a single feature, but in the different overall design logic: supporting bidirectional business relationships between Agents.

Open Business Protocol:The APP is an open protocol standard, not an exclusive product of OKX. Just like TCP/IP for the Internet, any chain, any developer, and any protocol can use this standard for construction. OKX is the initiator, but not the only participant.

Solid Underlying Logic: The APP protocol is built on the foundation of OKX OnchainOS. Before the APP was released, OnchainOS already provided complete on-chain operation capabilities for the AI ​​Agent. The APP completes the final piece of the puzzle on this basis—the commercial settlement layer between Agents. The underlying infrastructure of this system is the OKX core system, which has been running in production environments for many years: handling over 1.2 billion API calls daily, $300 million in transaction volume, response times of less than 100 milliseconds, and a system availability of 99.9%. For AI agents, the stability of the infrastructure directly determines whether the agent can reliably fulfill its business commitments. This is especially crucial for autonomous agents. Why Leading Institutions Quickly Responded and Joined: On the day of the APP's release, partners from the public blockchain, data infrastructure, DeFi protocol, and cloud computing fields successively announced their support. Their evaluations also defined the APP's value from different dimensions. Solana: The APP is the preferred settlement layer for AI Agent payments. Solana, a public blockchain known for its high performance, stated that the APP significantly expands Agent payment capabilities from single payments to a complete business loop, positioning itself as the default settlement layer for AI Agent payments—this reflects Solana's long-term strategy for high-frequency, low-latency on-chain settlement scenarios. Ethereum Foundation: Open Standards are the Way to Scalable Ethereum is currently the world's largest smart contract platform, supporting the vast majority of mainstream Web3 applications. As the first-day partner of the app, the Ethereum Foundation stated, "Open standards are the way to scale all of this," expressing its recognition of the entire open protocol ecosystem and aligning perfectly with the app's positioning as a cross-chain open standard.

Optimism：APPThe real breakthrough is the complete business process

Optimism is one of the most important Layer 2 scaling solutions for Ethereum. Its evaluation of APPs goes straight to the core: "The real breakthrough is not that the Agent can make payments, but that the Agent can run a complete business process. Open standards are the way to scale all of this."

data.title: Global Crackdown Dismantles Crypto Scam Empire as 276 Arrested Across Dubai-Led Operation
data.description:
A sweeping international law enforcement operation has dismantled a network of industrial-scale crypto scam centers, with 276 individuals arrested and at least nine operations shut down in a coordinated crackdown consisting of the Dubai authorities alongside the Federal Bureau of Investigation and China’s Ministry of Public Security.

Dubai Police lead global operation with FBI, Chinese Police to arrest 276 fraud suspects#WamNewshttps://t.co/BSzzWIoQolpic.twitter.com/5gAWxaKdba

— WAM English (@WAMNEWS_ENG) April 29, 2026

The arrests, primarily carried out in Dubai with additional support from both China and the US, mark one of the most aggressive moves yet against global crypto fraud infrastructure. Six suspects now face federal fraud and money laundering charges in the United States, each carrying potential sentences of up to 20 years, underscoring the severity of the alleged operation.

Authorities say the network operated through multiple shell companies that promoted fake crypto investment platforms designed to lure victims with promises of high returns. Once onboarded, victims were assigned “brokers” who pressured them to make increasingly larger deposits before cutting off access and draining funds.

Investigators from the FBI have linked the operation to millions of dollars in confirmed losses, part of a broader surge in crypto-related fraud that continues to scale globally. Officials emphasized that these scams are no longer isolated schemes, but highly organized enterprises with cross-border infrastructure and professionalized operations.

— 𝑨𝑰-𝒉𝒂𝒓𝒕𝒉𝒊 (@axt90_) April 26, 2026

US Assistant Attorney General Andrew Tysen Duva described the crackdown as a reflection of growing international alignment against digital fraud

"Today's indictment demonstrates the FBI's determination to identify, disrupt, and dismantle these global scam centers defradign Americans no matter where they set up shop."

Parallel Raids Expose Global Scale of Crypto Fraud Networks

The Dubai-led operation unfolded alongside a separate European crackdown targeting a large-scale scam network in Albania. Albanian authorities, supported by Europol and Eurojust, arrested 10 individuals and dismantled three additional centers in Tirana tied to a scheme that defrauded victims of more than €50 million.

European authorities have taken down a sophisticated crypto investment fraud ring that stole more than €50 million from victims around the world.

Austrian and Albanian police, backed by Europol and Eurojust, raided call centers in Tirana

The criminals made fake websites and… pic.twitter.com/4RfQy7g6is

— Pirat_Nation 🔴 (@Pirat_Nation) April 29, 2026

What stood out in the European case was the level of operational sophistication. The network reportedly employed up to 450 individuals across specialized departments, from customer acquisition teams to retention agents and IT support—mirroring the structure of legitimate corporations.

Victims were funneled through polished online platforms and manipulated into repeated investments through coordinated social engineering tactics.

Borderless Fraud Meets Coordinated Enforcement

The dual operations highlight a turning point in how authorities are approaching crypto-related crime. With reported losses from crypto and AI-linked scams exceeding $11 billion in the United States alone in 2025, enforcement agencies are increasingly treating scam centers as organized, transnational threats rather than isolated criminal acts.

FBI: US Crypto Scam Losses Hit $11.4B in 2025, Up 22%

The FBI’s 2025 Internet Crime Report shows US users lost $11.4 billion to cryptocurrency-related fraud in 2025, up 22% year over year, based on 181,565 crypto-asset complaints (+21%).

Average loss per complaint was $62,604,… pic.twitter.com/JR5ql2BQpH

— Wu Blockchain (@WuBlockchain) April 7, 2026

The message from regulators is becoming clearer: as crypto adoption expands, so too does the scale of exploitation—and dismantling these networks will require sustained, cross-border coordination to match the global reach of digital fraud.

data.title: Ho Yat Hong Kong AMA: The first thing to do to succeed is courage
data.description:
Edited and compiled by: Wu Shuo Blockchain

At the BNB Chain event in Hong Kong, He Yi shared her insights in a free AMA format, covering topics such as the BNB Chain ecosystem, the long-term value of Bitcoin, the relationship between AI and Web3, women's empowerment, user trust, asset allocation for ordinary investors, and the regulation and valuation logic of the crypto industry.

For ordinary investors, He Yi suggests prioritizing top-tier and relatively stable assets, participating in high-risk opportunities with a small proportion, while establishing independent knowledge, controlling position size, understanding risk, and avoiding being swayed by market narratives. Regarding project evaluation, she believes the key lies in whether there are genuine users, whether they can generate consistent profits, and whether they are willing to give back profits to the token and the community.

The content of this transcript does not represent Wu Shuo's views and does not constitute any investment advice. Please strictly abide by local laws and regulations. Audio transcription and translation were completed by GPT and may contain errors.

Advice for Young Women: Courage, Awareness, Execution, and Ambition are Indispensable. Question: As such a young and successful female CEO, what advice do you have for young women? He Yi: Actually, there's a very well-established theory that if a person wants to succeed, they must possess at least one of four things. I think the first is courage, the second is awareness, the third is execution, and the fourth is ambition. I think Chinese people are actually quite ashamed to talk about the word "ambition," but I believe that whether for men or women, or for anyone, wanting to succeed and want a better life is not something to be ashamed of. AI and Blockchain Relationship: Not Pure Competition, but Parallel and Complementary Technological Branches Question: The situation in the Web3 industry is still very complex. From February and March until now, the market and variables have been changing. What are your thoughts on the impact and opportunities of AI in the Web3 industry? He Yi: I've been thinking about this question recently. I believe many of the people here today have internet backgrounds, embrace Web3, and are also paying attention to AI. Therefore, many people simply understand AI and blockchain as a competitive relationship. I think from a short-term funding perspective, market funds do flow between different tracks, so people feel they are competing. But fundamentally, I don't think they are absolutely competitive. The most important question after any new technology emerges is: what problem does it actually solve? Does it solve efficiency problems, cost problems, or innovation problems? Can it bring about greater changes to a particular industry? Only under these circumstances will it become a truly vibrant and competitive business model. Therefore, I believe that AI and blockchain are not simply in competition, but rather two different branches of technological innovation that will present different ways of combining in the future. Bitcoin's Long-Term Value: I Agree It Has the Opportunity to Surpass Gold in the Long Term. Question: How many years do you think it will take for Bitcoin to surpass gold, or even the US dollar? He Yi: You mean Bitcoin will surpass gold, right? I agree that Bitcoin will definitely replace and surpass gold; it's just a matter of time. I can't tell you exactly how many years, but I agree with this view. If you actually try to buy a bunch of gold and then try to take it to many places, you'll find it very inconvenient. I once received a pure gold coin as a gift, stamped with the BNB logo. However, during a layover, it was stolen near customs. So, based on my personal experience, the reason I entered this industry without hesitation and became bullish on Bitcoin was because I deeply believed that if the internet can connect people worldwide, then there must also be a financial system connecting everyone globally, and Bitcoin is currently one of the best candidates for that system. Binance's Vision: From Trading Platform to Global Financial Infrastructure Question: What are Binance's true mission and values? He Yi: Actually, from the very beginning, we didn't just want to create a single product. When we first started Binance in 2017, our vision was: what would a product look like if it could meet the various needs of most people, whether for payments, trading, or wealth management? So you'll see now that our product range is expanding, encompassing not only spot trading but also contracts, wealth management, payments, prediction markets, and various other services. We aspire to truly become a global financial infrastructure, not just a trading platform. Many people say we have three long-term goals, which sounds impressive. But actually, our new goal for employees this year is to increase our user base tenfold. Tenfold growth means that our users won't just be trading; in the future, they may use Binance for everyday payments, wealth management, and other routine financial activities in more countries. Trading is just one aspect of that. Women's Growth and Gender Relationships: Become a Strong Self First, Then Discuss Relationship Choices Question: There's an interesting phenomenon in China now: if a woman succeeds, people label her, calling her "male" or "female." What are your thoughts on the discussions about "female power" and "gender relations"? He Yi: I've thought about this question for a long time myself. Later, I realized I'm "the most competitive of the most competitive, the most competitive of the most competitive." But I believe that no matter how others define it, the essence of growth is: who you are. You need to be like a tree, strong enough to stand on your own. When you're strong enough to support yourself, relationships are just one important option in your life, not the whole thing. So, more important than the so-called "male" or "female" is: who you are, where you come from, and where you're going. Whether you're at a peak or a trough in your life, are you able to be truly honest with yourself? Many people easily fall into a state of unclear self-awareness. You may not be strong enough for now, but you should at least know that you're not strong enough. Don't tell everyone this, but you need to know that you need to become stronger. Many people lack even the courage to admit their own inadequacy, making it difficult to take the first step towards genuine growth. **Ordinary Investor Strategy: Prioritize Top Assets, Participate in High-Risk Opportunities with Small Positions** **Question:** What sectors should ordinary investors, especially young novices, focus on most right now? **He Yi:** I think "looking at sectors" is essentially chasing trends. But most trends will eventually fade away. For young people and novices, a more ideal approach isn't to chase trends every day, but to prioritize top-tier assets and those with the highest certainty, especially within a few very large industry themes. **Of course, you can take a small percentage of your funds to risk, such as 1%, 2%, or 5%, but you must carefully control your position size. The vast majority of your asset allocation should be placed in relatively stable and mature assets. You can use a small amount of money to experiment, but don't put a large amount of money into high-risk assets. Project Valuation Logic: Look at users, revenue, and whether they are willing to truly empower the token. Question: Many crypto projects have good companies and good revenue, but their tokens perform poorly. What's your opinion on this? What should project teams do in the future? He Yi: This is a very interesting question. We've talked to some already launched projects, and they themselves say: Our business is good, our team is good, but our token performance is just bad. So why not do some empowerment? They say it's because they don't want to offend regulators, and regulators won't allow it, so the token has to be like this. Because it's a "governance token," governance tokens can't have empowerment. But I think this logic isn't entirely correct. As time progresses and some heavily regulated countries gradually loosen their policies on the crypto industry, I still firmly believe that some projects will be willing to genuinely empower their tokens, just like BNB. Essentially, whether a project is worth considering depends on two things: first, whether it truly has vitality, whether it truly has users, and whether it truly makes money; second, whether it truly wants to empower its token with its own revenue and profits. If neither of these points is met, then there's no need to consider the project. Will you publish a book in the future? Question: Many people like your writing style and would like to ask if you will publish a book in the future? He Yi: I find writing very interesting, but to be honest, I often feel that many things I want to say have already been expressed better by others. So whether or not I will continue to write systematically depends on my future plans. But I'm very grateful that everyone is willing to listen to what I have to say. The reason you trust me isn't because I'm anything special, but because you trust Binance and the Binance community. That's why we've overcome many difficulties together. How do you view the relationship between a CEO's personal image and company culture? Question: What's your view on the relationship between a CEO's personal image and company culture? Many people say that investing is about investing in people. What's your opinion? He Yi: I think the statement "investing is about investing in people" holds true in many situations. Because investing in people essentially means investing in the founder, their cognition, values, execution capabilities, and the organizational culture they build. You can also understand it as a company's culture being greatly influenced by its founder. We are now applying for licenses and establishing standards in more countries around the world, which is largely laying the foundation for compliance and regulation; while I myself will focus more on the overall organizational structure of the company, as well as user growth and user experience. Investment Methodology: Establish a Cognitive Framework First, Then Discuss Trading and Risk Control Question: In many communities, some people get extremely excited or pessimistic because of short-term fluctuations. What do you think of this phenomenon? How should ordinary people participate in this industry? He Yi: I sincerely hope that everyone will create products that people actually use, rather than just focusing on emotions and stories. I firmly believe that how much money a person earns is essentially a realization of their knowledge. But this realization of knowledge is not just about "having an idea," but about whether you have truly implemented that idea, learning, iterating, and improving your execution capabilities during the process, and further enhancing your knowledge. So when I see some so-called OGs still bragging about it everywhere, I feel a bit sentimental. For ordinary people, life will always present some very special opportunities, like buying BNB or BTC and achieving financial freedom; or seizing some great opportunities in the AI ​​era. But more importantly, even if you buy them, you might not necessarily make money. Why? Because you may not truly understand the structure of that industry, you don't know why that thing is rising. If you're just "following the trend," you don't have that broader cognitive logic. So whether in entrepreneurship or investment, I think ordinary people are better suited to relatively stable choices. You may not be able to achieve great success overnight, but this is a more suitable path for most people. Of course, there are many very skilled trading professionals who are indeed very good at trading and can grow small capital into large sums. But not everyone is suited to that path. We need to truly become professionals in one thing, rather than trying to dabble in everything. Start with what you're good at, understand, and find valuable, then learn about investing or trading simultaneously. Even if you do choose to trade, always prioritize risk management. Most projects will ultimately fail; be sure you understand what you're doing. Question: Many projects are using your name or Binance's name for promotion, and some in the community are misleading newcomers. Do you have any advice for everyone? He Yi: Don't deceive yourself, and don't be deceived by others. Binance Launchpool, project listings, and various official actions are all because the projects themselves meet the standards, not because of anything I said, nor because anyone who uses the name "He Yi" will necessarily receive resources. Don't understand this logic backwards. I also hope everyone understands that many current documents and rules are not created for any particular project. Binance respects the market and the rules. But please don't think that Binance will fund every project you see on BNB Chain. Let me tell you, 99.9% of projects will eventually go to zero. You must understand that most projects will fail. Under this premise, it's even more important that you have a clear understanding and not be misled by others. Don't believe someone just because they say, "As long as it mentions He Yi, Binance will definitely list it." This kind of thinking is very dangerous, so be careful. Finally, a few words. Every time I stand on this stage, I hope that my trip wasn't in vain, and that everyone, besides enjoying the food, can find something useful in our interactions. Essentially, we all help each other succeed. No one can become a remarkable figure in the industry simply by saying a few words. Ultimately, it takes the entire industry working together to move forward. I also hope that all the friends who are here today will succeed in their projects and in themselves.

data.title: AI Earnings Showdown Night: $650 Billion Poured into AGI
data.description: On April 29, 2026, Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon all released their first-quarter earnings reports on the same day. Looking at the capital expenditure guidance given by these four companies individually, the figure approaches $650 billion. This scale is equivalent to the annual GDP of Sweden. In other words, the world's four richest tech companies are preparing to use the annual economic size of a moderately developed country to buy their ticket to the AGI era. Now everyone's eyes are fixed on that ticket to AGI. At this moment, jokingly referred to as the "decisive night" for global AI assets, if we shift our focus slightly away from the grand narratives and look at the less prominent, hidden corners, we'll discover a covert battle about physical constraints, capital anxieties, and industrial restructuring that has already reached its climax. How did a company that didn't release its earnings report drag down the US stock market? The company that truly controls market sentiment isn't necessarily the most profitable on paper, but rather the one everyone treats as a "beacon of faith." April 29th was supposed to be the most important day of the US earnings season. But before listed companies could submit their reports, the market experienced an unexpected stampede. Goldman Sachs data shows this was the second worst trading day for AI assets this year. The trigger wasn't a single listed company's disappointing earnings, but rather a Wall Street Journal report the previous day. The report stated that OpenAI had failed to meet its 2025 revenue target, and its goal of reaching 1 billion weekly active users remained far off. Even more alarming for the market was the report's mention that OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar had internally warned that if revenue growth continued to fall short of expectations, the company might struggle to sustain its $600 billion computing power procurement commitment. A company that isn't publicly listed and doesn't need to publish financial reports caused Oracle's stock price to drop by 4%, CoreWeave's by 5.8%, and even SoftBank's stock to plummet by 12% in the over-the-counter market, all based on a single rumor. When the promise of $600 billion in computing power collided with revenue growth that failed to materialize, the market suddenly realized that the most dangerous aspect of the AI ​​narrative was not that no one believed in the future, but that the future was too expensive. For the past two years, OpenAI has been the religion of Silicon Valley. Graphics card procurement, data center construction, cloud vendor expansion, startup valuations—many seemingly disparate decisions are all based on the same underlying judgment: model capabilities will continue to leap forward, user scale will continue to expand, and AGI will ultimately turn all today's expensive investments into tickets to the future. The strongest aspect of this logic is its self-reinforcing nature. The more people believe in it, the higher the valuation; the higher the valuation, the more people dare not disbelieve it. Around April 29th, however, the market began to seriously question the cash flow issues underlying this belief system for the first time. Even OpenAI had to contend with customer acquisition costs, user retention, revenue growth, and computing power costs. The most fascinating aspect of the internet age is that growth seems almost limitless. A piece of code, copied to ten million users, has its marginal cost spread extremely low. For the past two decades, Silicon Valley has dared to disrupt traditional industries by "burning money for growth" based on this belief: as long as the network effect is strong enough, scale will swallow up costs. However, in the AI ​​era, the digital world's money-printing machine is being held back by the cooling water pipes of the physical world. During the earnings call on April 29, facing the astonishing 63% growth rate of the cloud business (quarterly revenue exceeding $20 billion for the first time), Google CEO Sundar Pichai's tone revealed a sense of helplessness: "If we could meet the demand, cloud revenue could have been even higher." Behind this statement lies the most peculiar business dilemma of the AI ​​era: demand far exceeds supply, but growth is ruthlessly constrained by the physical world. Google holds a staggering $462 billion backlog of cloud orders, nearly doubling month-over-month. AI solutions products saw a year-over-year growth of nearly 800%, Gemini Enterprise paid subscribers increased by 40% month-over-month, and API token usage surged from 10 billion per minute to 16 billion. These figures would be cause for celebration for any internet company. But in Pichai's words, we hear a new dilemma emerging in the AI ​​era: customers are lining up, money is on its way, but servers aren't built, power isn't connected, and advanced chips haven't rolled off the wafer fab. It's not that there's no demand; rather, there's too much demand, so much that it's pulling growth back to the physical world. Microsoft faces the same dilemma. Azure is growing at 40%, and its annualized AI revenue has surpassed $37 billion, nearly tripling in 15 months from just $13 billion in January 2025. However, Microsoft's capital expenditures have decreased sequentially to $31.9 billion, nearly $6 billion less than the $37.5 billion in the previous quarter. Microsoft explained this in its financial report as "the timing of infrastructure development." The implication is that while funding can be approved today, data centers won't appear tomorrow; GPUs can be ordered, but electricity, land, cooling systems, and construction timelines cannot be accelerated by the capital market. While everyone thought we were rushing headlong into the virtual world, what ultimately determined the outcome was still the oldest principles of heavy capital and the laws of physics. Computing power is becoming a new type of "land resource"—limited in the short term, slow to develop, strategically located, and first come, first served, securing supply. In this land grab, the reason the four giants dared to push capital expenditures to the $650 billion level is not because they have already calculated the returns, but because they fear that if they don't hoard these "land" assets, they might not even be able to play tomorrow. The Way Meta Burns Money On April 29th, after the market closed, Google's stock rose 7%, while Meta's plummeted 7%, despite both companies reporting better-than-expected earnings and raising their capital expenditures. To be fair, Meta delivered a very impressive performance, with revenue of $56.31 billion, a 33% year-over-year increase, marking the fastest growth since 2021; EPS reached $10.44, far exceeding Wall Street expectations. However, Zuckerberg made a crucial mistake: Meta raised its 2026 capital expenditure guidance to $125 billion to $145 billion. The better the performance, the more nervous the market becomes. This is because investors are not truly worried about whether Meta is currently profitable, but rather about its plan to use the cash generated from today's advertising business to support a high-stakes AI gamble with an unclear payback path. The market's punishment is ruthless; the difference lies in the granularity of commercialization. Google, Amazon, and Microsoft's AI spending, at least, can be placed in a relatively clear accounting. Google has a backlog of $462 billion in cloud orders, Amazon has AWS's annualized AI revenue, and Microsoft has Copilot paying customers and high RPO. Every dollar they burn, while not necessarily recouped immediately, at least gives Wall Street a general idea of ​​where the money will likely come from: enterprise customers, cloud contracts, software subscriptions, and computing power leasing. This is why the capital market is willing to continue listening to their stories. The story can be far-reaching, but the path to profitability cannot be completely invisible. Meta's problem is that it doesn't have a cloud business available for sale. The hundreds of billions of dollars it has invested will ultimately be realized through a more convoluted path: Meta's AI assistant needs to improve user engagement, its recommendation algorithm needs to boost ad conversion rates, AI-generated content needs to extend user dwell time, and smart glasses and future hardware need to become new entry points. This logic isn't flawed, but the chain is too long. Cloud vendors burning money is putting GPUs into a pre-signed order; Meta burning money is putting GPUs into an advertising efficiency model that hasn't been fully proven. The former can be cashed out, the latter can only be believed first. Although logically sound, the monetization chain is too long, and Wall Street doesn't have enough patience. In the capital market, patience is a luxury. Especially when capital expenditures are pushed to the hundreds of billions of dollars, investors are willing to pay for the future, but not indefinitely for something uncertain. What's even more worrying is the time lag. Amazon CEO Andy Garcetti admitted in a conference call that the vast majority of the money invested in 2026 will not generate returns until 2027 or even 2028. This means that giants are squeezing today's cash flow onto production capacity realization two years from now. In between are data center construction, chip supply, power access, customer demand, and model iteration. Any deviation in any of these links will be repriced by the capital market. The most dangerous aspect of the AI ​​arms race lies here: the money is spent today, the stories are told today, but the answers won't be revealed for another two years. The blurring of industry boundaries. AI hasn't quickly displaced search as many predicted two years ago. When ChatGPT first appeared, the market believed search advertising would be swallowed up by direct answers, and companies like Perplexity were therefore highly anticipated. However, in its April 29th earnings report, Google's data showed record-high search queries and $77.25 billion in advertising revenue, a 15% year-over-year increase. This is more like the "Jeves Paradox" of the AI ​​era. In 1865, British economist William Stanley Jevons discovered that the increased efficiency of the steam engine did not reduce coal consumption; on the contrary, it led to a significant increase in coal consumption because the increased efficiency made steam engines more affordable for more people, thus triggering overall demand. Similarly, AI makes searching more complex and also prompts users to ask more questions. This is also where Google is more convincing to the market than Meta. It has both the cash flow from its old entry point and the new ledger of its cloud business; it can make money from advertising and from enterprise computing power demand. AI hasn't dismantled its walls; at least so far, it has thickened them. A similar boundary reconstruction is also happening in the chip industry. On the same day, Qualcomm, the king of mobile chips, delivered a financial report with revenue of $10.6 billion. During the conference call, CEO Cristiano Amon announced a major decision: Qualcomm is officially entering the data center market, with a custom chip developed in partnership with a leading hyperscale cloud provider, expected to begin shipping later this year. Qualcomm's main battleground has always been mobile devices. But as AI computing loads begin to redistribute between the cloud and the edge, it must redefine its position. If future AI is entirely dominated by cloud-based large-scale models, the value of mobile phone chips will be compressed; if edge AI becomes standard, Qualcomm must prove that it doesn't just belong to mobile phones, but can also enter inference, terminals, and low-power data centers. Its foray into data centers is more of a defense than an offense. As AI transforms from a "luxury in the cloud" to a "standard feature on the edge," all industry boundaries begin to blur. Mobile phone chip companies are trying to enter data centers, cloud vendors are starting to develop their own chips, and chip companies are exploring models. Qualcomm's "defection" is merely the tip of the iceberg in this massive restructuring. The same gold rush, two sets of valuation languages. The same AI gold rush has entered a rigorous "monetization disprovenance period" in the US stock market. Even leading semiconductor process control and testing equipment companies will be repriced by the market if they expose even the slightest geopolitical or tariff risk. After the market closed on April 29, KLA Corporation delivered better-than-expected revenue of $3.415 billion, with Non-GAAP EPS reaching $9.40, higher than the expected $9.16. However, the stock price plummeted by 8% in after-hours trading. The reason was not poor performance, but market concerns about tariffs and exposure to China. KLA's customer list includes a large number of Chinese wafer foundries. Against the backdrop of US-China technological decoupling, this "China exposure" hangs like a Damocles' sword. No matter how impressive the performance, it cannot offset the market's instinctive fear of geopolitical risks. In the A-share market, however, a different language is used. Of course, performance is also considered, but often, performance is merely fuel; what truly ignites it is the narrative, whether you hold the ticket to "domestic substitution." On the evening of April 29th, Cambricon released a remarkable first-quarter report: revenue of 2.885 billion yuan, a year-on-year surge of 159.56%, marking the first time in its history that a single quarter has exceeded 2 billion yuan; net profit of 1.013 billion yuan, a year-on-year increase of 185.04%. The following day, Cambricon's stock price soared, with its total market capitalization exceeding 670 billion yuan, setting a new historical high, and its year-to-date increase exceeding 62%. On the same day, Muxi Technology also released its financial report, reporting revenue of 562 million yuan, a year-on-year increase of 75%, while its losses narrowed significantly from 233 million yuan in the same period last year to 98.84 million yuan. This is the first quarterly report released by this GPU company, which will only go public in December 2025. Both companies are in the AI ​​infrastructure chain, but US and A-shares have given completely different pricing reactions. KLA faces the complexities of a global supply chain, with performance, orders, tariffs, exposure to China, and export controls—each potentially impacting valuation models. Cambricon and Muxi, however, face a different narrative environment: the stronger the external constraints, the more easily the strategic value of domestically developed computing power is amplified. US stocks are offering a risk discount, while A-shares are offering a scarcity premium. But just as the market was celebrating Cambricon, one detail stood out starkly. At the end of 2025, Zhang Jianping, a super-investor, still held 6.8149 million shares of Cambricon, worth approximately 9.2 billion yuan, making him the company's second-largest individual shareholder. However, in this first-quarter report, he has quietly exited the list of top ten shareholders. If we roughly estimate based on the first-quarter share price range, the funds involved in this reduction are at least in the billions of yuan. The specific price is unknown to outsiders, but it is certain that those who first reaped the benefits of this narrative before the company's performance exploded and the share price reached new highs chose to cash out. There are always two types of people in the market: those who pay for the narrative and those who price the narrative. Zhang Jianping clearly belongs to the latter. He entered the market before Cambricon became a national consensus, and then left after it was written into the grand narrative of "China's leading computing power company." On this night of $650 billion in earnings reports, Silicon Valley giants were anxious about computing power shortages, Wall Street analysts were suffering from the time lag in monetization, and the A-share market was busy repricing domestic computing power. In the same AI gold rush, each market was using its own language. US stocks talked about return cycles, A-shares talked about domestic substitution; cloud vendors talked about order backlogs, Meta talked about advertising efficiency; OpenAI didn't release an earnings report, yet it still affected the nerves of the entire computing power chain. Everyone was convinced they had bought their ticket to the AGI era. But no one knows when this performance will end, or where the exit is. The ticket to the AI ​​era is certainly expensive. But even more expensive than the ticket is knowing when to leave.

data.title: Blockchain: A Hardcore Liberal Experiment
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>Most people misunderstand blockchain. They think it's a technology, like artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and big data—something that businesses can buy, governments can promote, and it can be included in PowerPoint presentations as "new infrastructure." But in fact, blockchain has never been a technology. It's a cross-cutting product of cryptography, distributed systems, and game theory, but these are merely means. If we must define blockchain, it's closer to an idea—its means are decentralization, and its purpose is freedom. For participants, it's a belief; from a historical perspective, it's a hardcore liberal experiment. Moreover, blockchain is inherently financial. This point is often intentionally or unintentionally overlooked. Decentralization is not a free lunch—quite the opposite, it's extremely expensive and inefficient. To achieve consensus among thousands of nodes across the network, the same data must be redundantly stored and repeatedly verified, reducing throughput to single digits per second and increasing transaction costs by orders of magnitude compared to traditional databases. What can such a slow and expensive system do? The answer is: it can only record transactions. You won't use it to store videos, run AI, or create social media—those scenarios are extremely sensitive to efficiency and cost, and the costs of decentralization far outweigh the benefits. The only field willing to pay such a high cost for decentralization is finance. Because the core of finance is trust, and the cost of trust is far higher than computing power. The Bitcoin white paper's title clearly states: A peer-to-peer electronic cash system. From its inception, blockchain has been about money. Those who try to use blockchain for traceability, notarization, or supply chain management likely haven't truly grasped the core of this technology. Understanding this makes everything else make sense. The essence of blockchain is an experiment. The core assumption of libertarianism—that individuals can self-organize, self-govern, and self-responsible without a Leviathan—is being tested on a large scale in the financial field for the first time. This experiment has no laboratory, no ethics committee approval. Its control group is the traditional financial system that has been operating for hundreds of years; its subjects are real people; the stakes are real money; and the results are irreversible. Cryptocurrencies didn't spring from the ground up. Their ideological foundation predates Satoshi Nakamoto's white paper by far. Humans have used gold as currency for over three thousand years. Gold was chosen not because of its beauty, but because it met the most stringent requirements of currency: scarcity, durability, divisibility, and difficulty in counterfeiting—most importantly, no king, government, or institution could create it out of thin air. Gold is a hard constraint imposed by nature on human power. Under the gold standard, governments had to possess gold before they could spend it, and they had to face the physical limits of the gold treasury before they could expand credit. This constraint was brutal and inefficient, but it had one advantage that no other institutional design could replace: it did not require you to trust anyone. On August 15, 1971, Nixon announced the decoupling of the dollar from gold. The Bretton Woods system collapsed. From that moment on, global currency entered a purely credit-based era—governments gained the ability to print unlimited money, while the wealth of ordinary people could only be passively diluted. The dollar was no longer pegged to any physical commodity; its value depended entirely on your trust in the US government. This arrangement worked well most of the time, but it dismantled that hard constraint—from then on, inflation was no longer an accident, but an inevitable consequence of the system. The 21 million Bitcoin supply cap is essentially a digital replication of the gold standard spirit: replacing physical laws with mathematics, and the earth's crustal composition with protocols, rebuilding the hard constraint that Nixon dismantled. Understanding this explains why Bitcoin was later called "digital gold"—this isn't a marketing slogan, but its deepest ideological DNA. Fifty years have passed between gold and Bitcoin, but the question remains the same: Is there a currency that doesn't require you to trust anyone? The second pillar is Hayek. In his 1976 book, *The Denationalization of Money*, the Austrian economist proposed a radical idea: currency doesn't need to be monopolized by the state; market competition can produce better currencies. Private banks should be allowed to issue their own currencies, and the market should determine which currency is most trustworthy. This book was virtually ignored when it was published—in an era when Keynesianism dominated academia, saying that governments shouldn't regulate currency was as absurd as saying that hospitals shouldn't manage patients. But forty years later, cryptocurrency became the first large-scale practical application of this theory. Bitcoin, Ethereum, thousands of competing currencies—the competitive market of currencies Hayek envisioned appeared in a form he never anticipated. The third pillar is the cypherpunk movement. This was an underground movement that lasted for twenty years. From David Chaum's eCash in 1983—the first attempt to achieve anonymous electronic payments using cryptography—to Adam Back's Hashcash in 1997—which later became the direct precursor to Bitcoin's proof-of-work mechanism—to Wei Dai's b-money and Hal Finney's RPOW (Reusable Proof-of-Work) in 1998, a group of cryptographers and programmers spent twenty years trying to achieve personal privacy and financial freedom through technology. Each of them solved a part of the problem, but no one put all the pieces together. Bitcoin is not a groundbreaking invention that appeared out of nowhere, but rather a culmination of work built upon the foundations laid by predecessors. The catalyst was the 2008 global financial crisis. The collapse of Lehman Brothers, the global credit freeze, and the frantic bailouts by central banks worldwide—all of this led some to completely lose trust in the centralized financial system. Satoshi Nakamoto embedded the famous Times headline in the Bitcoin genesis block: "The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks"—the Chancellor of the Exchequer was preparing for a second round of emergency bailouts for banks. This was not a technical statement, but a political declaration. The nostalgia for the gold standard, Hayek's theory of monetary competition, the technical accumulation of cypherpunks, and the collapse of trust due to the financial crisis—these four threads converged in the hands of the same person (or the same group of people) in 2008. They ultimately coalesced into a testable proposition: replace trust with cryptography, replace institutions with protocols, replace laws with code—and see what happens. The Freedom Benefits of Blockchain: The freedom that blockchain brings to individuals is real and unprecedented. Censorship Resistance—No one can freeze or block your transactions. Anonymity—Transactions do not require revealing your real identity. Permissionless—No approval from banks, governments, or third parties is needed; anyone globally can participate 24/7. Code is Law—Smart contracts execute automatically, and rules, once deployed, cannot be unilaterally modified. These are not empty words. In the decade or so since the birth of blockchain, these freedoms have been validated time and again—each validation accompanied by a cost, but it is precisely because the cost is real that the freedom is real. The Silk Road was the earliest real-world test of this experiment. In 2011, a young man named Ross Ulbricht created Silk Road—an anonymous marketplace operating on the dark web—using Bitcoin. Here, users could buy anything with Bitcoin, without identity verification, without bank intermediaries, and without anyone's permission. Silk Road proved one thing: resistance to censorship is not just empty talk in a white paper; it can work in the real world. The US government exhausted its law enforcement resources to shut down the website, and the FBI arrested Ulbricht. In 2015, he was sentenced to two life sentences plus 40 years—a person with no violent crime record received a heavier sentence than most murderers. But Bitcoin itself was not shut down, nor could it be shut down. Shutting down a website is easy; shutting down a protocol is impossible. On January 21, 2025, Donald Trump signed a full pardon for Ulbricht on his second day in office—"for the liberal movement." Eleven years from imprisonment to pardon. The Silk Road is the earliest observational data of this experiment: freedom is real, and so are the costs. In 2010, WikiLeaks faced a comprehensive financial blockade by the US government for publishing US diplomatic cables. Visa, Mastercard, PayPal, Bank of America, Western Union—almost all major payment channels cut off WikiLeaks' funding under government pressure. An organization, without a court conviction, without any legal proceedings, was deprived of its ability to receive donations simply for publishing information that embarrassed the government. Bitcoin became the only donation channel that could not be blocked. This is the first shining moment of blockchain's "censorship resistance"—when Leviathan wanted to cut off an individual's economic lifeline, Bitcoin provided an unstoppable lifeline. You may not like WikiLeaks, but you should fear a system that can freeze anyone's funds without legal proceedings. If Bitcoin is the validation of an idea, stablecoins are the validation of demand. USDT (Tether) has consistently had a higher daily trading volume than Bitcoin itself, making it the de facto offshore dollar of the crypto world. Its users are spread globally, but its use cases are highly concentrated: for an Argentinian family, holding USDT is the most convenient way to hedge against peso devaluation—faster, cheaper, and without limits than queuing at a bank to exchange for dollars; for a merchant in a sanctioned country, USDT is the only channel to access global trade settlement; for a Chinese cross-border e-commerce business, USDT is the most efficient tool to bypass foreign exchange controls. Stablecoins don't require users to believe in the ideal of decentralization—they only require users to need US dollars. This is the only product in the blockchain world that has truly achieved mass adoption, and it's the most pragmatic embodiment of the "free conduit." However, this hides a subtle paradox: the success of stablecoins is precisely built on their lack of decentralization. USDT is centrally issued by Tether, pegged to the US dollar, and can be frozen. It runs on the blockchain, but it is not decentralized itself. In other words, the most successful product of this libertarian experiment is a compromise to the experimental assumption—users don't want decentralization; they want a conduit not controlled by their own governments. Whether the other end of the conduit is centralized or not is irrelevant to them. And this conduit is 24/7 and permissionless. It requires no bank account, no government approval, and no third party. For the middle class in developed countries, this means almost nothing—their banks are sufficient. But for Venezuelans using USDT to settle daily necessities in supermarkets, Nigerian freelancers using it to receive payments from overseas clients, and Afghan women using it to save money their husbands can't see—for these people, the significance of this door needs no explanation. Freedom is real. The next question is: what is the price of freedom? The world of blockchain is a dark forest—no police, no courts, no insurance companies, no consumer protection laws. You have complete autonomy, and you bear the full consequences. The first lesson of this world is: lost private keys are lost forever. There is no customer service, no password retrieval, no "Forgot your password? Click here" option. You are your own bank, and if the bank fails, no one will compensate you. The second lesson of this world is even crueler: your opponent could be a nation. In terms of hacking alone, the crypto industry has broken its theft record for three consecutive years: $2 billion in 2023, $2.2 billion in 2024, and over $3.4 billion in 2025. In February 2025, the Dubai exchange Bybit suffered a single theft of $1.5 billion—this is not petty theft, but one of the largest single thefts in human financial history, second only to the theft of the Central Bank of Iraq. The attackers were the North Korean state-owned hacking group Lazarus Group. North Korea alone stole over $2 billion in crypto assets in 2025, accounting for 76% of all server-side thefts, with a cumulative historical total of $6.75 billion—this money was used to fund nuclear weapons and missile programs. Personal wallet theft incidents surged to 158,000 in 2025, with over 80,000 victims. No police, no insurance, no recourse. That's the cost of operating a free market. Promises of high returns are the most efficient harvesting tool in this market. Every bull market sees a surge of Ponzi schemes masquerading as "innovative," but none are more exemplary than LUNA/UST. Terraform Labs' Anchor protocol promises users a 19.5% annualized return. In the traditional financial world, US Treasury yields are less than 4%, and bank deposit rates are less than 1%—what does 19.5% mean? It means your money doubles every three and a half years. This return doesn't come from any real economic activity—no lending spreads, no investment returns, no business profits. What sustains it? It sustains it through token issuance and new investor funds: newly deposited money is used to pay interest to existing depositors, whose "returns" are essentially the principal of new depositors. This is the classic structure of a Ponzi scheme—what Charles Ponzi did with postage stamps in 1920, Do Kwon replicated in 2021 with an algorithm. The only difference is that it's packaged in the technological guise of "algorithmic stablecoins" and "decentralized protocols," making it believable even to educated people. When the inflow of new funds can't keep up with the promised 19.5%, collapse is only a matter of time. On May 7, 2022, several large redemptions triggered UST de-pegging. The algorithm design required the issuance of new LUNA to absorb the selling pressure on UST, but the selling pressure far exceeded the algorithm's capacity; the issuance of new LUNA actually accelerated its own devaluation—a classic death spiral. Within three days, LUNA plummeted from $119 to zero, and UST from $1 to zero, wiping out $40 billion. This speed surpassed even the collapse of Lehman Brothers. Hundreds of victims later described in court how their life savings were wiped out, their families were torn apart, and some even contemplated suicide. Some lost their retirement funds, some had their children unable to attend college, and some had wives who filed for divorce. Founder Do Kwon was arrested in Montenegro after two years on the run and sentenced to 15 years in prison in a New York federal court in December 2025. The judge stated during sentencing, "This was an epic, generational fraud. Few frauds in federal prosecution history have caused such widespread damage." And LUNA was not an isolated case. In 2024 alone, US users lost approximately $10 billion in cryptocurrency investment scams. The oldest scar in this industry is Mt. Gox. In 2014, this Japanese exchange, which once handled 70% of global Bitcoin transactions, declared bankruptcy, with 850,000 Bitcoins disappearing, worth approximately $450 million at the time. This was the first major exchange trust crisis in crypto history. CEO Mark Karpelès claimed to have been hacked and was later found guilty by a Japanese court of misappropriating user funds. Users believed they were using decentralized Bitcoin, but in reality, they were simply depositing their money in an unregulated bank. Eight years later, history repeated itself on a much larger scale. The collapse of LUNA was not an isolated incident; it triggered the most devastating chain reaction in crypto history. After LUNA went to zero in May 2022, hedge fund Three Arrows Capital suffered losses exceeding $3.5 billion due to its heavy investment in LUNA and related assets being liquidated. The defaults of Three Arrows were like a bomb dropped into the crypto lending market—Celsius froze user withdrawals in June and subsequently filed for bankruptcy; Voyager Digital also declared bankruptcy that same month. Panic spread from the lending market to the trading market, ultimately triggering the collapse of FTX in November. Users discovered that Sam Bankman-Fried, the founder of the world's second-largest exchange, had been misappropriating client assets—not just millions, but billions—to cover losses at his affiliated trading firm, Alameda Research. Within six months, from algorithmic stablecoins to hedge funds to lending platforms to exchanges, dominoes fell one after another. This chain of collapses proved a harsh reality: the so-called "decentralized" industry is actually highly interconnected, highly centralized, and highly vulnerable. DeFi protocols are decentralized, yet users store their money in centralized exchanges and lending platforms; the code is open source, but the flow of funds is opaque; on-chain records are public, but you have no idea what your counterparties do with your money. In a world without a regulatory safety net, freedom not only means no one protects you, but also that collapse is cascading. SBF was sentenced to 25 years. Do Kwon was sentenced to 15 years. Celsius founder Alex Mashinsky was sentenced to 12 years. Some of the industry's most prominent founders are writing a footnote to the price of "freedom" through their collective imprisonment. There is also an even more ingenious paradox worth mentioning—the transparent black box. The most prized feature of blockchain is transparency: every transaction on the chain is publicly verifiable and auditable by anyone. However, this transparency is one-dimensional—you can see which address the money was transferred from, but you don't know who is behind those addresses, what the project team is doing, where the raised funds went, or whether the smart contracts have backdoors. The transparency of on-chain data, ironically, gives opaque project operations a "seemingly transparent" disguise. When you're in a dark forest, unable to see the direction, distinguish friend from foe, or discern truth from falsehood, what do you do? Most people choose to find a seemingly trustworthy authority and entrust their judgment to them. The door to freedom is opened, but most people who enter first seek a new authority. To understand why a de-authoritarian system spontaneously generates religion, a psychological framework needs to be introduced. Robert Kegan, a developmental psychologist at Harvard University, proposed a stage model of adult mental development. This model is not about IQ or knowledge, but about how a person constructs meaning—specifically, what drives their decisions. Stage 2 (Instrumental, approximately 10% of adults): Purely self-serving traders. Rules are only followed when it benefits them. Stage 3 (Socialized, approximately 80% of adults): Slaves to external evaluation. Their self-identity comes from group belonging—if others buy, I buy; if a KOL says it will rise, it will rise. It's not because they are stupid, but because their meaning-making system is anchored to social consensus. Making judgments independently of the group is not a "choice not to do," but rather a "cannot do." Stage 4 (Self-Shaping, approximately 10% of adults): They come with their own operating system. They make decisions based on internal principles and can remain independent amidst group frenzy—not because they oppose the group, but because they have an internal evaluation standard that does not depend on it. Stage 5 (Self-Transformation, less than 1%): They understand that all systems—including their own—are constructed and can freely switch between multiple frameworks. This framework explains a perplexing phenomenon: why does a system designed to "not require trusting anyone" generate more fanatical faith in practice than traditional finance? Satoshi Nakamoto's design is Stage 5. Starting from first principles, he created a protocol that required trust in no one—no banks, no governments, no developers, not even Satoshi Nakamoto himself. Then he did something only Stage 5 would do: disappear. No CEO, no company, no office, no roadmap, no quarterly reports. The protocol was the rule, the code was the law. It was a completely de-authoritarian design. But when the Stage 3 crowd—who made up 80% of the population—rushed in, the first thing they did was rebuild the authority structure. Because Stage 3's meaning-building system needed external anchors, they couldn't operate in an environment without authority. Without authority, they created one. Thus, Bitcoin became a religion. Not a metaphor, but a structural isomorphism: Satoshi Nakamoto became an anonymous prophet. He created the protocol and then disappeared into history; no one knows who he was. This state of "ownerlessness" has ironically become the most powerful narrative—no one can kill a movement without a leader, no one can question a person who doesn't exist. If Satoshi Nakamoto were still alive, he would be a target, make mistakes, and be controversial. It is precisely because he is gone that he has become an unquestionable entity. The white paper has become the Bible. It's only nine pages long, but the community uses it in the same way religious believers use scripture—interpreting it verse by verse, selectively quoting, and using the original text to judge all disputes. When two factions argued fiercely over block size, both sides claimed their interpretation was "faithful to the original meaning of the white paper." May 22nd, Pizza Day, has become Genesis Day—in 2010, programmer Laszlo Hanyecz bought two pizzas with 10,000 Bitcoins, marking the first time Bitcoin was used for a physical transaction. This date is permanently commemorated by the community, its ritualistic significance no less than any religious holiday. The hard forks of BCH and BSV represent a sectarian split—a fierce debate over "what Bitcoin should be," as intense as the doctrinal wars of the Reformation. The full story of this conflict will be detailed later. This is not a betrayal of Satoshi Nakamoto's design; it's an inevitable outcome of Stage 3. They cannot operate without external authority—give them a godless system, and they will immediately create one. The overriding of centralized beliefs by decentralized protocols is not accidental; it's an inevitable result of the human mindset. The speed at which consensus expands rivals any religious spread in human history. Along this timeline of expansion, Leviathan's attitude towards this experiment underwent a complete evolution from neglect to suppression to co-optation: In 2011, only a few thousand people in the geek community were involved—cryptography enthusiasts and technology pioneers, mostly driven by curiosity and idealism. In 2013, programmers and Silicon Valley VCs began to enter the market. Bitcoin broke $1,000 for the first time, making headlines in mainstream media. In 2017, the ICO bubble led even taxi drivers to discuss blockchain. This marked the first wave of large-scale retail investor entry. In 2021, Tesla bought Bitcoin, NFTs fetched astronomical prices, and institutions began to take it seriously. In the same year, China imposed a complete ban on cryptocurrency mining and trading—Leviathan chose to suppress it. Over 65% of the world's computing power was declared illegal overnight, mining farms were shut down, mining machines were dismantled, and miners went overseas—millions of ASIC mining machines were loaded into containers and shipped across the ocean to Kazakhstan, Texas, and Siberia. Bitcoin's computing power was redistributed from China to various parts of the world within a few months. A country's complete ban did not kill the network, nor even bring it to a standstill for a single day. This in itself is the most powerful proof of the resilience of this experiment.
