In January 2024, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) approved a Bitcoin spot ETF—Leviathan chose to incorporate it. Traditional financial giants such as BlackRock, Fidelity, and Invesco entered the fray. This was a landmark moment when consensus moved from the public to the system, but it may also be a turning point for the spirit of libertarianism: Bitcoin being incorporated into the regulatory framework of the traditional financial system means that Leviathan was not there to destroy it, but to tame it. When your Bitcoin is housed in an ETF, held by traditional brokerages, and traded on the New York Stock Exchange, do you truly still possess the freedom to "not trust anyone"? In 2025, the power struggle at the sovereign level will begin. China's suppression and the US's co-opting—two paths leading to the same destination: Leviathan will not allow an uncontrolled financial system to remain forever outside the system. But neither path has killed the network. Each new layer of believers enters not because they understand decentralization, but because "everyone else is buying." From geeks to sovereignty, fourteen years have traversed a path that traditional religions took millennia to complete. The most important ritual in the Bitcoin religion is the halving. But to understand halving, you must first understand miners. The security of the Bitcoin network isn't guaranteed by cryptography alone; it requires real physical investment. Miners use electricity to power specialized chips to perform massive hash calculations, competing for the right to record each block. The winner receives the block reward—newly minted Bitcoins. This is essentially an investment: investing in electricity and hardware costs, betting that the produced Bitcoins will sell for enough to cover costs and generate a profit. This investment has fueled a fifteen-year-long arms race in computing power. Early miners used laptop CPUs to mine Bitcoin, which was quickly replaced by GPUs, then FPGAs, and finally specially designed ASIC chips—each generation of hardware had hundreds or thousands of times more computing power than the previous one, rendering the previous generation of hardware instantly obsolete. Around this chain of computing power competition, a capital-intensive industry sprang up: mining machine manufacturers (Bitmain, Canaan Creative), mining farm operators, and mining pool service providers. Mining farms chased the world's cheapest electricity—from hydropower during the rainy season in southwest China and thermal power in Inner Mongolia, to natural gas in North America, geothermal energy in Iceland, and surplus power in Central Asia. Mining pools aggregated computing power scattered globally, reducing the income fluctuations of individual miners; essentially, they were funds for computing power. Before the Chinese ban in 2021, China controlled more than 65% of the world's Bitcoin computing power. Understanding the miner's economic model—income equals block rewards plus transaction fees, and costs equal electricity costs plus hardware depreciation—is essential to understanding what the halving means. Satoshi Nakamoto wrote an unchangeable rule into the protocol: every 210,000 blocks (approximately four years), the block reward for miners halves. It was 50 coins in 2009, 25 in 2012, 12.5 in 2016, 6.25 in 2020, and 3.125 in 2024. This is a deflationary curve hard-coded into the code, which no one can modify—unless you can convince a majority of nodes on the network to agree to change the rule, which is politically virtually impossible. Technically, halving is the core mechanism of Bitcoin's monetary policy. It ensures that the total supply limit of 21 million coins is eventually approached but never exceeded. The rate of Bitcoin issuance is predetermined, completely transparent, and not subject to anyone's will—this is the fundamental difference between it and fiat currency. Sociologically speaking, however, halving has become a pilgrimage cycle for believers. Before each halving, a wave of narratives about a "halving rally" emerges in the community—reduced supply inevitably leads to price increases, and a new bull market is on the horizon. History seems to validate this: after the 2012 halving, Bitcoin rose from $12 to $1100; after the 2016 halving, from $650 to $20,000; and after the 2020 halving, from $9000 to $69,000. What about after the 2024 halving? From $64,000 to $126,000—an increase of approximately 100%, which sounds like a lot, but compared to the previous three halvings that saw increases of tens of times, the amplitude is visibly compressed. This data is the most powerful evangelistic tool for the Bitcoin faith. But believers spread this data not because they understood the mechanism by which supply contraction affects prices in monetary economics, but for a simpler reason: "Prices went up after the last halving." Stage 3 logic doesn't need causality; it only needs the appearance of historical patterns. Halving turns technical parameters into prophecies and protocol rules into dogma. But the other side of halving is dangerous. This will be discussed in detail later. The Cycle of Narratives A bull market is like igniting a pile of firewood. The total amount of firewood is limited—it corresponds to a pool of potential new buyers. Each piece of firewood lit represents someone being drawn into the market by the group's consensus. The size of a fire doesn't depend on the fire itself, but on how many unburnt pieces of wood remain. The moment the flames are at their fiercest—the day with the highest trading volume—is precisely when the last batch of wood has just been ignited and the remaining fuel has been exhausted. Therefore, the moment the fire is at its strongest is the moment it's about to go out. Why is the peak of a bull market always marked by the highest trading volume? The answer isn't in finance, but in psychology. Why are all the pieces of wood ignited? Because Stage 3 individuals cannot help but be ignited—group consensus is their oxygen. When everyone around them is making money, their social media feeds are filled with reports of profits, and the headlines are all about record highs, the Stage 3 brain doesn't analyze fundamentals; it sends an irresistible signal: I should be there too. The halving here acts as an igniter. The supply contraction narrative that occurs every four years provides the first spark for a new round of firewood. But what truly determines the size of the fire is not the halving itself, but how many unburnt pieces of wood remain. The history of the crypto world is a history of narrative iteration. The Bitcoin craze laid the foundation of faith for the entire industry, but faith needs constant renewal—each new narrative essentially seeks new points of excitement within the consensus framework pioneered by Bitcoin. And each narrative doesn't appear out of thin air: they each address a real problem and create a new illusion. The Altcoin Era (2011–2014): Copying is Innovation. Bitcoin proved the feasibility of decentralized currency, but its code was open source—meaning anyone could copy it, change a few parameters, and start a new chain. Litecoin changed the block time from 10 minutes to 2.5 minutes, and Dogecoin removed the total supply limit and replaced it with a Shiba Inu icon. These "innovations" seem absurd today, but at the time they addressed a real problem: Bitcoin was too expensive and too slow; the market needed alternatives. The illusion lies in the fact that changing parameters does not equal improvement, and copying code does not equal copying consensus. The vast majority of altcoins ultimately go to zero because they copied Bitcoin's code but failed to replicate Bitcoin's network effects and first-mover advantage. Ethereum (2015): A Leap in Infrastructure. If Bitcoin proved the feasibility of "decentralized ledgerkeeping," Ethereum upgraded the blockchain from a ledger to a platform. A Russian-Canadian teenager named Vitalik Buterin wrote the Ethereum white paper at the age of 19, proposing a seemingly simple idea: what if the blockchain could not only record "who transferred how much money to whom," but also record and execute arbitrary program logic? This is smart contracts. Ethereum, with its Turing-complete Virtual Machine (EVM), transformed "code is law" from a slogan into infrastructure. From then on, the blockchain was not just a ledger, but a globally shared, unstoppable computer. The emergence of Ethereum made all subsequent narratives—ICOs, DeFi, NFTs, MEMEs—technically possible. It's not part of a narrative iteration, but the foundation of all subsequent narratives. However, "code is law" faced its ultimate test in 2016. A smart contract called The DAO was hacked, resulting in the theft of 3.6 million ETH. The Ethereum community voted to hard fork and roll back the transactions, thus creating Ethereum Classic (ETC). This event essentially acknowledged the fact that code is not law; consensus is. The ICO era (2017): White papers equal fundraising. Ethereum's smart contracts lowered the barrier to entry for issuing tokens from "knowing how to write blockchain underlying code" to "knowing how to write a white paper." The ERC-20 standard allowed anyone to create a token in minutes, making ICOs (Initial Coin Offerings) the most efficient fundraising machine in history. It addressed a real problem: traditional venture capital had too long a cycle and too high a barrier to entry, leading to the monopolization of excellent projects by capital. ICOs democratized investment rights—anyone could participate in early-stage projects using ETH. The illusion lies in the fact that lowering the threshold for fundraising also lowers the threshold for scams. A single PowerPoint presentation can raise tens of millions of dollars, and even taxi drivers are discussing blockchain. The vast majority of ICO projects never deliver any product after receiving funding. The 2018 crash was inevitable: when fundraising has no constraints, money will flow to those who are best storytellers, not those who are best at doing the work. The DeFi Era (2020): Rebuilding Wall Street on the Chain. DeFi (Decentralized Finance) attempts to answer a deeper question: since blockchain is inherently financial, why do financial activities still rely on centralized exchanges and lending platforms? Protocols like Uniswap, Compound, and MakerDAO replace financial intermediaries with smart contracts, enabling permissionless lending, trading, and stablecoin issuance. These are genuine innovations. The illusion lies in "yield." The essence of liquidity mining is subsidizing users with token inflation; annualized returns of hundreds or even thousands of percentage points do not come from real economic activity, but from the dilution of funds and tokens from later entrants. When subsidies stopped and users withdrew, the TVL (Total Value Locked) of most DeFi protocols plummeted by over 90%. DeFi proved the feasibility of on-chain finance, but it also proved that returns without real demand were merely a variation of Ponzi schemes. The NFT Era (2021): Everything Can Be On-Chain. NFTs (Non-Fungible Tokens) allowed non-financial users to understand blockchain for the first time. Previously, the blockchain narrative revolved around "money"—currency, payments, and finance. NFTs expanded the narrative to "ownership"—a picture, a piece of music, a game item—all could be registered and traded on-chain. Bored Ape and CryptoPunks turned avatars into identity symbols, allowing artists to bypass galleries and directly reach buyers for the first time. It addressed a real problem: the long-standing lack of ownership of digital content, and the severe exploitation of creators in the platform economy. The illusion lies in the fact that the blockchain only records a correspondence between an ID and an address. What that ID represents—a picture, a piece of music, or nothing at all—is not recorded on the chain, nor is it guaranteed. Ownership does not equal value. You own a JPG image with an on-chain identifier, but anyone can right-click and save the same image. When speculation recedes, the shelf life of social currencies is much shorter than imagined. RWA Narrative (2023–): Back to Reality. After experiencing the altcoin bubble, the ICO fundraising scams, the DeFi yield illusion, and the NFT ownership illusion, the crypto world seems to be starting to "sense." RWA (Real-World Assets) attempts to move traditional assets such as government bonds, real estate, and equity onto the blockchain, using blockchain infrastructure as a distribution channel for traditional finance. On-chain assets finally have real cash flow backing—but at the cost of returning value anchoring to off-chain. You buy a tokenized US Treasury bond on the chain, but the underlying compliance, custody, and redemption all rely on centralized intermediaries. Is this a "victory for decentralization" or a "return to centralization"? The answer is probably the latter. The MEME Era (2024–): No More Pretense. Each previous narrative disguised itself to varying degrees as "technological innovation" or a "paradigm revolution." MEME coin is the first narrative to completely tear off the disguise. There's no technology, no product, no roadmap, and not even any pretense of "doing things." An animal icon, inexplicably popular, with a market capitalization of billions. But MEME coin is the most honest—those who buy it aren't buying assets, but a sense of participation, belonging, and the emotional experience of "I was there too." Emotional value outweighs practical value; this is the true product. Looking back at this evolutionary chain, a disturbing trend emerges: the technological content of each narrative is decreasing, the speculative purity is increasing, and the cycle is shortening. Altcoins at least require compiling a blockchain, ICOs require writing a white paper, DeFi requires deploying smart contracts, and NFTs require creating a set of diagrams—but with MEME, none of that is needed. An ICO project that could be popular for months in 2017 might only have a lifespan of a few hours in 2024. What does this curve point to as the end result? Perhaps the crypto world is honestly demonstrating its unchanging nature: it's a speculative market where consensus pricing dictates the price, and narratives are merely the entry ticket to each bull market. So what is a bear market? It's waiting for new fuel to grow. A new generation grows up, accumulating enough capital, but hasn't yet experienced the lessons of a crash. The cycle won't disappear, but the amplitude will compress—after the old fuel has burned once, some people will no longer be ignited by the same narrative (a few people have completed the leap from Stage 3 to Stage 4 through losses), while the total amount of new fuel is diminishing at the margin. The previous chapter chronologically covered seven narratives. Now, let's step back and examine the structure behind the timeline. There's a fact the blockchain world is reluctant to face: aside from Bitcoin, Ethereum, and a few leading platform coins, the operating logic of the vast majority of crypto assets is no different from that of internet celebrities—their popularity is inexplicable, lacking logic and fundamentals, purely attention-driven. An animal icon, a market capitalization of billions—you can't explain it using any rational framework. MEME coin simply pierced this veil. But in fact, most altcoins, most ICO tokens, and most DeFi governance tokens share the same underlying logic: attention equals market capitalization, and narrative equals fundamentals. Cryptocurrencies aren't a category, they're a framework—almost all crypto assets, except for a very few with real cash flow or non-fungible network effects, can be understood using this framework. This reveals a fundamental nature of the crypto market: traditional value investing frameworks fail here. The three pillars of value investing—intrinsic value, margin of safety, and long-termism—each require cash flow as a foundation. To assess a company's worth, you need to predict its future free cash flow and then discount it back to today (DCF). To find a margin of safety, you need to know how much something worth one dollar is worth and then buy it at half price. To be a long-term investor, you need to be confident that time is on your side—good companies accumulate value over time, and bad companies are eliminated by time. But crypto assets lack cash flow. Bitcoin doesn't generate profit, Ethereum's gas fees go to miners and validators, not token holders, and most tokens don't even generate revenue. Without cash flow, there's no intrinsic value to calculate; without intrinsic value, there's no margin of safety to measure; without a margin of safety, long-term holding isn't "value investing," but merely another form of faith. Without a foundation, all the analytical frameworks above are castles in the air. So, in a market where value investing fails, what determines the price? First, the attention economy. In a market without cash flow, profit, or verifiable fundamentals, attention is the only scarce resource. A project's core competitiveness isn't technology or product, but the ability to acquire and maintain attention. KOL endorsements, community operations, airdrop incentives, meme dissemination—these aren't marketing tactics; they are the product itself. People buying influencer-endorsed coins aren't buying assets, but a sense of participation, belonging, and the emotional experience of "I was there too." Emotional value outweighs practical value. Secondly, the illusion of liquidity. The seemingly booming market capitalization and trading volume of trending cryptocurrencies are built on extremely fragile liquidity. A token with a nominal market capitalization of $1 billion may only have a few million in actual buying pressure—the actual liquidation depth of most tokens is far lower than their nominal market capitalization. When holders sell off in a concentrated manner, the price can drop to zero within minutes. This is not an anomaly in the market; it is the norm. The "value" of most assets in the crypto world has never truly existed; it is merely an illusion briefly reached between buyers and sellers. Understanding trending cryptocurrencies means understanding the true nature of the crypto market: it is an attention-driven market where consensus pricing prevails, and narratives are merely the entry ticket to each bull market. This is itself an observation of an experiment—when you give the market complete freedom and remove all regulatory constraints, the equilibrium state that the market ultimately converges to is not rational pricing, but an attention casino. Will Bitcoin Collapse? This is a dangerous judgment, but I feel it's necessary to say it. Let's go back to the early days of Bitcoin. In the early days of Bitcoin, Satoshi Nakamoto wrote a 1MB block size limit to prevent spam attacks. This was a temporary solution and was never intended to be permanent. However, a protracted civil war erupted in the Bitcoin community over this line of code. The scaling proponents argued that Bitcoin should become a global payment system and that the block size must be increased—the white paper's title clearly states that it's a "peer-to-peer electronic cash system." Conservatives argued for prioritizing decentralization, even at the expense of performance—larger blocks raised the barrier to entry for running full nodes, ultimately limiting transaction verification to large institutions and rendering decentralization meaningless. The technical debate quickly morphed into a battle of beliefs, with both sides claiming to be the true "faithful to Satoshi Nakamoto's original intent." Ultimately, the Bitcoin Core development team seized control of the code repository merging process, effectively hijacking Bitcoin's technological path under the guise of decentralization. The small-block approach prevailed. The cost was that Bitcoin's TPS (transactions per second) cap was permanently locked in single digits. The consequences of this decision were far-reaching. Early on, major companies like Microsoft, Dell, Newegg, and Steam integrated Bitcoin payments, marking the closest Bitcoin came to realizing its vision of "electronic cash." But as on-chain congestion worsened, transaction fees soared, and confirmation times lengthened, users voted with their feet, and one company after another abandoned Bitcoin payments. The Lightning Network was highly anticipated, but years later, it has yet to become a mainstream payment solution—it solved the on-chain congestion problem but introduced new complexities and user experience issues. On August 1, 2017, the scaling proponents finally opted for a hard fork, creating Bitcoin Cash (BCH). This wasn't a technical upgrade, but a formal split within the community—a doctrinal war about "what Bitcoin should be." One side said Bitcoin should be currency, the other said it should be gold. One side wanted functionality, the other wanted a narrative. Ultimately, the narrative won. This is a key observation from this experiment: even in the most decentralized systems, power spontaneously recentralizes. Controlling the codebase means controlling the definition; controlling the definition means controlling sovereignty. Decentralized protocols cannot prevent centralized governance. The Bitcoin white paper is titled "Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System"—ironically, no one buys anything with Bitcoin today. What's left of something that has lost its functionality? Only a narrative. Thus, Bitcoin transformed into "digital gold." But the "digital gold" analogy doesn't hold up to scrutiny. Gold's scarcity comes from physical laws—the chemical properties of element 79 on the periodic table are determined by the Big Bang and supernova nucleosynthesis, which no one can change. But Bitcoin's scarcity comes from its code protocol, and code can be copied. In fact, it has been copied countless times—LTC, DOGE, forks like BCH and BSV, and countless faster, more feature-rich competing chains. Bitcoin's only non-replicable aspect is its network effect and first-mover advantage, but network effects are not physical laws; they can be eroded, migrated, and replaced. Gold doesn't need to worry about a "Gold 2.0" one day; Bitcoin cannot. Gold is backed by massive non-financial demand—jewelry consumption, electronics industry, aerospace, medical devices, and tens of thousands of tons of strategic reserves held by central banks worldwide. This demand provides a solid price floor for gold: even if all speculators exit simultaneously, gold will still have buyers and users. Bitcoin lacks this safety net. It is not a raw material for any industrial process, nor a necessity for any consumer scenario. All of Bitcoin's demand comes from a single source: the belief that it will become more expensive in the future. Once this belief wavers, no fundamental demand can support the price. The ultimate question in the consensus game is: where is the last buyer? From retail investors to institutions to sovereign wealth funds, the path of consensus expansion seems unstoppable, but the entry of each new type of buyer shrinks the pool of remaining potential buyers. When everyone has entered the market, who will be left holding the bag? There is also a more fundamental crisis: the collapse of the security model. As mentioned earlier, halving is a metronome of faith. But from an engineering perspective, halving is a time bomb. The assumption supporting Bitcoin's security model is that as block rewards decrease, transaction fees will steadily increase due to the scarcity of block space, eventually replacing block rewards as the main source of income for miners. But reality has proven otherwise—with each halving, miners' block reward income is halved, while electricity costs and hardware depreciation remain unchanged. Profit margins are squeezed, marginal miners exit the market, the network's hashrate decreases, and security is consequently reduced. Meanwhile, users are leaving due to high transaction fees, and businesses are abandoning the service due to poor user experience. On-chain transaction volume and fee revenue have not grown as expected. When block rewards eventually approach zero, and transaction fees cannot support sufficient computing power, who will guarantee network security? Bitcoin may not collapse in the way people imagine—not overnight to zero, but slowly losing its security foundation with each halving, until a black swan event triggers it at a critical point. Each halving reduces its time to find the answer by four years. After stripping away the veneer of faith and dismantling the narrative mechanism, a more fundamental question must be addressed: how many people truly demand freedom? The real demand for blockchain technology exists. A large number of people are using it—for cross-border fund transfers under capital controls, hedging in gray areas, privacy protection, and censorship-resistant payments. A Chinese businessman needs to transfer money abroad, an Argentinian family needs to hedge against currency devaluation, an individual in a sanctioned region needs access to the global financial network, and there are also gray-area money laundering and various unspeakable transactions. These needs are rigid and real. Users don't care about the ideal of decentralization, but they need this channel. Stablecoins are the core carrier of these needs—USDT's daily transaction volume on the Tron chain has exceeded the total cross-border payments of many medium-sized countries. However, these needs share a common characteristic: niche, hidden, and with low ceilings. How many people need to circumvent capital controls? How large are the scenarios requiring anonymous transactions? What percentage of the global population is affected by financial sanctions? This is a real but limited market. Those who need it are already using it. The real problem lies with the industry. The term "small circle" doesn't refer to users—users have genuine needs. "Small circle" refers to industry professionals. The scale of development and funding of exchanges, public chains, DApp developers, and investment institutions in the crypto industry are configured according to the logic of the mass market. Billions of dollars have been invested in payments, social media, games, content platforms, and supply chain management—all based on a flawed assumption: ordinary people need decentralization. Ordinary people don't. 99% of people would rather use WeChat Pay than manage their private keys. Convenience takes precedence over sovereignty. Freedom has a cost, and most people are unwilling to pay that cost—they don't even see it as a loss. Those who need it are already using it, and they don't need much infrastructure—a wallet, an exchange, and a stable blockchain are enough. The demand ceiling is low, but the industry is investing in it as if it were an infinite ceiling. This is the essence of "self-indulgence within a small circle"—practitioners are building a pipeline for a niche need as if it were world-changing infrastructure. The mismatch in scale is the root cause of almost all the huge investments that have failed in this industry. It's not that the technology is bad, it's not that the team is bad, it's that the market simply isn't big enough. This also explains why the business models of the crypto industry have ultimately converged into three: exchanges, collecting fees and listing fees from speculators; stablecoins, collecting seigniorage and interest rate differentials—Tether uses user-deposited dollars to buy US Treasury bonds, generating over $13 billion in net profit in 2024 with a team of less than 100 people, surpassing most Wall Street investment banks and becoming the most profitable company in the crypto industry, bar none; and quantitative trading, profiting from market fluctuations and market making. These three business models serve three different types of people: exchanges serve speculators—a large group whose goal is to make money, who don't care about decentralization or libertarianism, and who need volatility and leverage; stablecoins serve a niche group of users with real needs—cross-border transfers, hedging, and gray market transactions—who need a conduit; and quantitative trading serves the market's liquidity. None of them are serving a "decentralized future for a billion people." The only truly profitable business is the infrastructure that serves speculators and those with real needs—not the grand narratives that try to get ordinary people to embrace blockchain. But this niche need is enough. Even if Bitcoin's fate, as predicted in the previous chapter, slowly erodes its security foundation with each halving, blockchain as infrastructure will not disappear. Stablecoins don't need Bitcoin, Ethereum doesn't need Bitcoin, and value transfer networks don't need to be tied to a single chain. Bitcoin is the flagship product of this experiment, but not the only one. Blockchain technology may be rudimentary, applications may be scarce, and speculation may be rampant—but it has opened a door: for the first time, individuals possess the ability to store and transfer value without relying on any institution. This is humanity's weapon against Leviathan. In a world where governments can freeze bank accounts, central banks can print unlimited amounts of money, and financial institutions can impose arbitrary restrictions, a value transfer network that no one can shut down has inherent significance. Even if the most successful product of this experiment is a compromise—a centrally issued, decentralized alternative to the dollar—it still proves one thing: Leviathan cannot completely control the flow of value. In 2021, China completely banned cryptocurrency mining and trading. The Bitcoin network didn't stop for a second. Over 65% of the global computing power was wiped out by the ban, millions of mining machines migrated across continents, and within months, the computing power regrouped in North America, Central Asia, and Northern Europe. A country used all its administrative power to shut down a network, yet not a single block was missing. This is perhaps the most powerful illustration of the phrase "cannot be shut down." Once this door is opened, it can never be closed again. Like all real experiments, it is full of failures, noise, and side effects. Most projects will go to zero, most narratives will be forgotten, and most participants will lose money. This market is not lacking in fanaticism, scams, self-righteous idealists, or bloodthirsty speculators. But the value of an experiment never lies in the success of every step, but in whether it opens up a possibility that didn't exist before. Just as printing disrupted the church's monopoly on knowledge, and the internet disrupted traditional media's monopoly on information, blockchain shakes the financial intermediaries' monopoly on the flow of value. This disruption may take decades to show its full consequences, but the direction is irreversible. Pandora's box has been opened. Returning to the initial question: What exactly is blockchain? More than a decade has passed, and the answer is becoming increasingly simple: it is a hardcore liberal experiment. This experiment, using fifteen years and trillions of dollars, has answered an ancient question of political philosophy for humanity—where are the boundaries of freedom? The answer is: freedom is real, expensive, and only a few can wield it. But as long as this few exist, this door can never be closed. True freedom is not about owning a decentralized wallet, but about having a mind that is not dictated by the emotions of the masses.</div></div>

data.title: A Currency War That Was Halted: The US-China Pricing Power Struggle Behind RWA
data.description:
Abstract

This paper focuses on the key turning point of the phased tightening of RWA regulation in China, systematically analyzing the underlying financial structural constraints, and further revealing the deep mechanism by which RWA has evolved from a technological innovation into a tool for competing for monetary pricing power in the global system. The study finds that China's phased tightening of RWA is not aimed at the technology itself, but at the three structural spillover risks it may trigger: asset-backed securitization issuance, expansion of cross-border capital flows, and embedding into the dollar pricing system. In the regulatory evolution path, RWA has gradually risen from a fintech pilot to a macro issue involving financial stability and monetary sovereignty. At the same time, the global RWA market exhibits a highly consistent dollarization path: stablecoins dominate the settlement system, US Treasury bonds form the yield anchor, and DeFi undertakes liquidity allocation, resulting in non-dollar assets being systematically incorporated into the dollar pricing framework on-chain. This structure has led to RWA not only failing to weaken the dollar system, but also strengthening its central position in global asset pricing.

Against this backdrop, the path to RMB internationalization clashes structurally with the RWA system: RMB assets struggle to achieve closed-loop pricing and liquidity construction within the existing on-chain financial structure, instead facing spillover risks of "asset RMBization and pricing USDization." Hong Kong acts as an institutional buffer within this system, but the overall USD-dominated landscape remains unchanged. Ultimately, this paper proposes three evolutionary paths: full USDization, an offshore RMB system, and a dual-currency structure model. Among these, the dual-track structure of RMB assets + USD liquidity truly possesses the potential for institutional balancing, but its establishment presupposes the coordinated restructuring of the regulatory, capital, and monetary systems. The conclusion indicates that the ultimate competition for RWA is not limited to technical architecture but further depends on the ability to reshape the monetary and regulatory systems; essentially, it is a global redistribution of asset pricing power. Note: The term "suspension" in this paper does not refer to explicit policy prohibition, but rather to project contraction, slowed progress, and temporary stagnation at the level of regulatory enforcement and market behavior. Keywords: RWA dollarization pricing system, asset securitization and regulatory restructuring, RMB internationalization and structural conflicts, cross-border capital flows and stablecoin mechanisms, global competition for asset pricing power. The Policy Logic and Realistic Constraints of China's Suspension of RWA Issuance. 1.1 From Encouraging Technology to Prudent Tightening: The Real Path of RWA Regulatory Evolution in China. Simply interpreting the current phase of contraction or slowdown in China's RWA issuance as a regulatory tightening may underestimate the institutional level adjustment reflected in this change. Combining the progress of various RWA projects in mainland China and Hong Kong over the past two years, a relatively consistent regulatory evolution trend can be observed: China's attitude towards RWAs did not suddenly shift, but rather, as RWAs gradually acquire cross-border asset pricing and capital allocation functions, regulators began to significantly strengthen prudential constraints. Therefore, while publicly available policy texts do not contain a single, explicit prohibition, a convergence of results has emerged in regulatory implementation and market behavior: the pace of most RWA projects has slowed significantly, and some projects have entered a period of stagnation. To understand this change, it is necessary to observe it within a longer timeframe rather than focusing on individual policies. This timeline reveals a crucial shift: Regulatory focus is undergoing three leaps—from technological feasibility to financial attributes, and then to funding pathways and monetary impact. In the early stages (2019–2021), regulators' attitude towards blockchain was clear and positive. Whether in supply chain finance, electronic invoices, or judicial evidence preservation, blockchain was seen as an infrastructure tool for improving efficiency. However, there is an implicit premise in this stage: Blockchain can transform processes, but it cannot change the financial structure. Entering 2022–2023, the market began to show obvious changes. Some institutions tried to tokenize assets such as accounts receivable and rental income rights and introduced investors to participate. In this stage, RWA began to transition from technology application to financial product. The regulatory attitude also changed accordingly. In 2024, the Securities Association of China issued the "Guidelines for the Application of Blockchain in Asset Management (Draft for Comments)", which for the first time clearly proposed that assets should be real, data should be traceable, and supervision should be penetrable. [2] The core significance of this document is that it did not negate RWA, but rather drew a boundary: all on-chain assets must return to the existing financial regulatory system and cannot form an extra-system financial system. The real turning point appeared at the end of 2024 to 2025. During this phase, we clearly felt a shift in regulatory focus during project implementation, moving from the assets themselves to the structure: regulators in multiple regions issued guidance prohibiting the tokenization of domestic assets through overseas SPVs; some Chinese securities firms slowed down or suspended RWA-related business in Hong Kong under this guidance; and several technology platforms proactively scaled back their stablecoin and on-chain financing initiatives. These changes, while not documented in unified public documents, produced a highly consistent result at the market level: RWA projects shifted from rapid exploration to overall contraction. Entering 2025–2026, this trend further intensified, manifested not only in guidance but also in a systemic strengthening of cross-border asset tokenization review and stablecoin risk warnings (such as increased requirements for overseas tokenized securities registration). In actual compliance communication and regulatory feedback, regulatory focus has gradually shifted to three core dimensions: whether it constitutes a de facto securities issuance, whether it creates a new channel for cross-border capital flows, and whether it leads to the inclusion of RMB assets in the USD pricing system. Especially in some tokenized projects with overseas issuance and domestic asset backing, regulators are no longer discussing the technical path, but have directly returned to a more fundamental question: does this structure change the way funds flow and the logic of currency pricing? This change indicates that RWA's position within the Chinese regulatory framework has shifted from a fintech application issue to a structural issue involving macro-financial stability and monetary system security. Looking back at the current regulatory tightening from this perspective, its meaning becomes clearer. What regulators are blocking is not RWA itself, but the three key paths behind it: asset splitting and quasi-securitization, stablecoin-driven cross-border capital flows, and dollar-anchored asset pricing. These three paths constitute the core operating mechanism of the current global RWA system. Therefore, a more accurate assessment can be made: China is not against developing RWA, but rather it does not allow it to grow at its current stage by circumventing regulation through cross-border flows and dollar pricing. This definition also provides a crucial premise for the following discussion: the constraints RWA faces in China likely lie in whether it violates the boundaries of financial structure, capital flows, and monetary sovereignty. 1.2 The problem isn't on-chain, but in disguised financialization: Why RWA naturally crosses regulatory red lines. In actual project implementation, a very typical misjudgment is that many teams believe the problem lies in the blockchain or token, but what regulators are truly concerned about is never the product form, but rather the essence of the financial structure. If we break down the RWA projects that have appeared in the Chinese market, they are essentially highly concentrated in three types of assets: (1) Accounts receivable and factoring assets (similar to credit) (2) Real estate rental income rights (similar to REITs) (3) Supply chain finance claims (similar to ABS) These assets have one thing in common: In the traditional financial system, they are all heavily regulated assets. Once the RWA structure is implemented, three key changes occur: (1) Assets are split: Assets that were originally single debt or real estate income are split into tradable shares, a step that is close to securitization. (2) Investors are amplified: From the original banks and trusts, they become potential global investors. (3) Liquidity is activated: On-chain transactions give assets quasi-secondary market attributes. After these three steps are combined, structurally, RWA is not simply asset digitization, but more like an on-chain securitization process. This is one of the areas most sensitive to Chinese regulators. More importantly, a core principle of China's regulatory system is "penetrating regulation." This means that regardless of whether it's packaged as a token, points, or digital certificate, as long as it features fundraising and profit distribution, it's essentially a financial product. This is why some RWA projects, even those that don't use public blockchains at all, have still been halted. A deeper contradiction: Shadow banking and cross-border capital. Further investigation reveals that RWA is not just a securitization issue, but also touches upon two systemic risks: First, the restructuring of shadow banking. Structurally, RWA is extremely similar to past trusts, asset management, and ABS: underlying asset packaging, establishment of SPVs, tiered issuance, and investor subscription. The only difference is that the issuance vehicle has changed from an asset management plan to a token. However, from a regulatory perspective, this structure is easily circumvented: capital adequacy constraints, information disclosure rules, and investor suitability management. Therefore, in some regulatory analysis frameworks, this type of structure is considered to possess characteristics of shadow banking, meaning it may form a credit expansion and risk transmission mechanism outside the system. Secondly, the technical channel for capital outflow. A more sensitive issue lies in cross-border transactions. When the RWA structure is combined with a stablecoin system, its funding path may evolve as follows: domestic assets → overseas SPV, investors subscribe using USDT/USDC, and returns are distributed in USD. This may form a new structural path: domestic asset returns are converted into USD-denominated equity through stablecoins and achieve cross-border circulation on-chain. Under this structure, the focus of regulatory attention is on whether a closed loop of cross-border funds has been formed that is not included in the existing foreign exchange and securities regulatory system. The relevant regulatory documents in 2026 have clearly required that the filing and review of tokenized securities issued overseas and backed by domestic assets be strengthened to prevent disorderly expansion. [1] From this perspective, the problem with RWA is not the asset, but that it has changed the path and pricing system of funds. 1.3 The same asset, two systems: the structural differentiation between the tightening of regulation in the mainland and the promotion in Hong Kong If we shift the perspective from the mainland to Hong Kong, we will see a very typical institutional misalignment. On one hand, there's tightening regulation and prudent constraints in mainland China; on the other hand, there's Hong Kong's proactive advancement. This divergence isn't a policy conflict, but rather a division of labor within the system. Looking at the Hong Kong market, its policy orientations show a clear difference. Over the past two years, Hong Kong's actions in the RWA (Reliable Weapon Asset Management) field have been very clear: promoting a stablecoin licensing system (such as the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's draft stablecoin regulatory framework), promoting pilot programs for green bonds and government bond tokenization (such as the Hong Kong government's green bond tokenization issuance project), and encouraging institutions to issue on-chain assets. Essentially, Hong Kong is building a system: an on-chain financial market centered on the US dollar (or pegged to the US dollar). Why are there two paths within the same country? The reason is not complicated; essentially, it stems from two sets of financial logics: This means that under different institutional environments, the regulatory attributes and market positioning of the same asset exhibit significant differences. Even without considering regulatory attitudes, from a practical operational perspective, RWA in mainland China faces four fundamental constraints: (1) Capital account not open → unable to freely raise funds across borders (2) Data export restricted → conflict between on-chain transparency and regulation (3) Inconsistent asset ownership system → insufficient legality of on-chain transactions (4) Unclear legal attributes of tokens → lack of investor protection These problems cannot be solved in the short term. This is precisely why regulators choose not to allow for unchecked experimentation, but rather: before the system is perfected, they choose to strengthen risk constraints rather than allow for unchecked experimentation. 1.4 Retrospective Analysis of Two Typical Projects: Why Did They Appear Compliant Yet Ultimately Fails to Implement Themselves? Over the past two years, we have closely followed the progress of several RWA projects. A very typical observation is that most RWA projects have strong compliance logic in the design phase, but often encounter obstacles at key stages during implementation. The core reason is not a problem in a single aspect, but rather that as the structure progresses, the project gradually encounters higher-level regulatory constraints. We can abstract this process into a typical path: Asset digitization → Separation of revenue rights → Introduction of investors → Liquidity design → Formation of cross-border funding channels → Regulatory reclassification. The two cases below essentially follow the same path, only stopping at different stages. The first project comes from the supply chain finance sector, with underlying assets being accounts payable from core manufacturing enterprises. The project team was very cautious from the outset: the asset side selected core enterprise debt with clear payment terms and extremely low default rates; structurally, an SPV was introduced for isolation; and even at the risk control level, repurchase and shortfall compensation mechanisms were set up. From a traditional financial perspective, this is a standard ABS-like structure, almost impeccable. The real change occurred on the funding side. To improve financing efficiency and cost advantages, the project chose to issue tokens through an offshore SPV and introduced Southeast Asian funds, using stablecoins for subscription. It was at this moment that the nature of the project fundamentally changed; it was no longer just a supply chain financing tool, but became a USD-denominated asset securitization product for global investors. Regulatory focus also shifted accordingly. Initially, the discussion revolved around the authenticity of assets and risk control models, but soon, regulators began repeatedly questioning several issues: How do funds flow back after entering the blockchain? How are the identities of overseas investors identified? Does the profit distribution form a closed loop for cross-border funds? These questions have technical explanations, but no compliant answers exist within the regulatory framework. Ultimately, this project was deemed an unapproved cross-border asset securitization. The core reason was not that the assets were non-compliant, but rather that the funding path had deviated from the controllable scope of the domestic financial regulatory system. In contrast, the second project's starting point was more straightforward: splitting rental income from office buildings in first-tier cities and selling it to investors through tokens. From a business logic perspective, this is almost the easiest to understand and the most attractive model for RWA: stable cash flow, clear assets, and predictable returns. However, precisely because it was so standard, problems surfaced more quickly. During the project's development, several key design elements gradually overlapped: to increase participation, the investment threshold was lowered; to enhance liquidity, a secondary trading mechanism was introduced; and to increase attractiveness, the profit distribution schedule was moved forward. Each design is reasonable on its own, but when combined, they form a very clear structure—a divisible, tradable, publicly offered revenue-generating product. In a regulatory context, this is almost equivalent to securities. China already has clear institutional arrangements for such assets: if real estate is the underlying asset, funds are raised from the public, and profits are distributed, the only compliant path is to enter the public REITs system. Any structure that bypasses this system, no matter how technologically advanced, is essentially an off-the-books securitization. It is precisely at this point that the project had no room for adjustment and ultimately had to be terminated. Looking at these two cases together reveals a crucial but easily overlooked commonality: the projects failed not because of non-compliance, but because they could not be incorporated into the existing regulatory system. In other words, the problem with these projects isn't what they did wrong, but that they attempted to do something the current system isn't prepared to handle. The first case stopped at the cross-border capital flow stage, triggering foreign exchange and capital flow regulations; the second case stopped at the revenue rights structure stage, triggering securities issuance regulations. But the essence of both is the same: when RWAs simultaneously possess the three elements of asset splitting, investor expansion, and liquidity design, their nature changes from a product realization issue to a structural issue involving the financial system and regulatory framework. It is precisely at this moment that seemingly compliant structures are dismantled and categorized within the existing regulatory framework. This is why, based on practical experience, the most common way for RWA projects in China to terminate is not through explicit rejection, but rather through: inability to complete registration, inability to obtain channel support, and inability to establish a closed-loop capital flow. Ultimately, these projects were passively terminated or suspended during the compliance process. From this perspective, the value of these cases lies not in their failure, but in their clear revelation of one thing: at the current stage, the real constraint on RWA in China is not on the asset side, nor on the technology side, but on whether it changes the way funds flow and the pricing currency of assets. Once the answer is yes, it becomes difficult for the project to continue. These cases show that the focus of regulatory restrictions is not on asset digitization itself, but on the changes in the financial structure it triggers. What regulators are truly concerned about is not whether the technology is feasible, but whether, after assets are tokenized, they will circumvent the existing financial regulatory system and form extra-system finance. 1.5 Summary: Tightening is not a denial, but a proactive defense against the risk of shifting pricing power abroad. Abstracting the analysis in this chapter, we can summarize it as follows: China's cautious attitude towards RWA stems not from insufficient technological maturity, but from the fact that its potential impact has touched upon three key constraints in the financial system: asset pricing, capital flows, and monetary sovereignty. More specifically: at the financial level, it may restructure shadow banking; at the capital level, it may open cross-border channels; and at the monetary level, it may allow RMB assets to be priced in US dollars. Therefore, this round of policy adjustments is closer to a proactive risk control arrangement than a simple market contraction. The core issue is not whether to develop RWA, but whether, without complete reliance on the US dollar system, an independent asset pricing and capital circulation mechanism can be established. Pricing Mechanism and Global Dominance of Dollar Assets in RWA 2.1 Why is RWA Naturally Dollarized: The Pricing Structure is Asymmetrical from the Start From a technical perspective, RWA possesses neutral attributes—theoretically, assets from any country can be on-chained, split, and traded. However, in the actual market, since its early project launches, RWA has exhibited a significant dollar-dominated characteristic, particularly evident in mainstream issuance and funding structures. The root cause of this phenomenon lies not in the assets themselves, but in their embedded pricing and liquidity structures. Looking at existing projects, the price of RWA assets is not solely determined by underlying cash flow, but is driven by a combination of factors: return, risk, and liquidity. For example, in US Treasury RWA projects (such as short-term US Treasury products issued by Ondo Finance), although the yield comes from US Treasury interest, the actual pricing still depends on the on-chain stablecoin funding demand and redemption liquidity. Another example is some emerging market real estate RWA projects; even if the underlying rental income is stable, insufficient on-chain dollar liquidity often necessitates offering higher yields to complete financing. In the traditional financial system, these three layers typically complete a closed loop within the same monetary system. However, in RWA, the latter two layers, especially the liquidity premium, are almost entirely determined by on-chain USD funds. This means that even if the underlying assets originate from non-USD economies, their pricing process still needs to be expressed and matched through the USD liquidity system. In other words, RWA does not achieve asset globalization, but rather that asset returns are incorporated into a unified pricing framework centered on the USD. 2.2 How the USD is Embedded in RWA: Three Layers of Control from Settlement Instrument to Yield Anchor Further analysis reveals that the USD does not play a single role in the RWA system, but rather controls the entire system through three layers—settlement, pricing, and liquidity.

First layer: Stablecoins, locking of settlement currencies

According to statistics from RWA.xyz and CoinGecko in April 2026[3], about 83% of RWA-related transactions were completed through stablecoins (caliber: on-chain transactions and protocol interactions), mainly USDT and USDC. The role of stablecoins in the RWA system is not only that of a settlement tool, but also the core carrier for revenue pricing and capital anchoring. It effectively determines: the currency of investor returns, the method of asset valuation, and the benchmark unit of the market. Once the RWA project completes fundraising through stablecoins, its liabilities are denominated in US dollars, thus passively converting asset returns into US dollar returns during the distribution process. This step completes the first layer of dollarization. The second layer: US Treasury yields, a globally unified pricing anchor. In all RWA product designs, a variable that is rarely publicly emphasized but always present is the risk-free rate. In the current system, this anchor is only the US Treasury bond. This means that all RWA assets are implicitly compared to US Treasury yields. The result is a very clear sorting logic: (1) Lower than US Treasuries → Unattractive (2) Slightly higher than US Treasuries → Low-risk assets (3) Much higher than US Treasuries → High-risk assets This mechanism incorporates global assets into the same dollar yield curve. The third layer: The DeFi system, the ultimate allocator of liquidity. If stablecoins solved how money gets in, and US Treasury bonds defined how yields are priced, then the DeFi system determines where funds ultimately flow. The current mainstream path can be broken down as follows: RWA assets are put on-chain → used as collateral in lending protocols → attract stablecoin liquidity → amplify funds through collateralization/re-collateralization. In this process, assets no longer exist as independent yield generators, but are embedded in the on-chain collateral and liquidity system, participating in the overall capital cycle. The core assets of this network remain stablecoins (USD) and collateralized assets such as ETH. This means that the liquidity source of RWA assets essentially depends on the size and activity of the on-chain USD stablecoin liquidity pool. Thus, a complete closed loop is formed: Stablecoin (settlement) → US Treasury (pricing) → DeFi (distribution) 2.3 From Model to Reality: How the US Dollar Gains Pricing Priority in RWA When the above three layers are superimposed, the pricing logic of RWA undergoes a key change: Wherein: (1) CF: Underlying asset cash flow (2) r_UST: US Treasury yield (risk-free rate) (3) RP: Credit and default risk premium (4) LP: On-chain liquidity premium (determined by stablecoin supply and demand) Compared with the traditional DCF model, this pricing structure has two core differences: In the traditional DCF In traditional currency systems, the risk-free interest rate is typically based on the local currency yield curve; however, in RWA, this variable is uniformly replaced by the US dollar interest rate (US Treasury yield). Traditional asset liquidity is primarily determined by local market depth, while RWA asset liquidity depends on the on-chain supply of US dollar funds. The result is that asset pricing power is partially transferred from the local market to the global US dollar liquidity system. This difference has led to a clear stratification in the actual market: (1) US Treasury RWA products: supply falls short of demand (2) Emerging market assets RWA: financing difficulties The essential reason is not the quality of the assets, but whether they are naturally embedded in the dollar system. From the three actual project case studies, this logic has been repeatedly verified. (1) Ondo Finance: By putting short-term US Treasury bonds on the blockchain, it constructs on-chain quasi-risk-free assets, attracting stablecoin funds to prioritize allocation, thereby strengthening the dollar yield anchor. (2) MakerDAO: By incorporating real assets (such as US Treasury bonds) into the DAI collateral system, it makes stablecoin credit directly dependent on dollar assets, structurally expanding the influence of the dollar system. (3) BlackRock BUIDL: By putting dollar assets on the blockchain with institutional-grade funds and connecting on-chain liquidity with a compliant structure, it further embeds traditional dollar assets into the Web3 system. Although these cases have different paths, the conclusions are highly consistent: These cases show that RWA has not weakened the dollar system, but rather expanded its pricing and liquidity boundaries under the new technical architecture. 2.4 The overlooked reality: Why are non-dollar assets systematically undervalued? In practice, a very common but easily misjudged phenomenon is that non-dollar assets are not of poor quality in the RWA system, but are repriced in a dollar-centric pricing system. This problem is mainly reflected in two aspects. First, exchange rate fluctuations directly affect the actual returns of investors, especially in the structure of non-dollar assets raised in stablecoins. For example, in Southeast Asian real estate RWA projects, even if the local currency rental income reaches 6%–8%[10], after conversion to US dollars and considering exchange rate fluctuations, the actual returns of investors decrease significantly, leading to increased financing difficulties. When an asset denominated in RMB or other currencies raises funds through a US dollar stablecoin, the actual returns of investors need to be deducted from exchange rate fluctuations. If there are expectations of devaluation, investors will actively demand higher returns as compensation, which directly depresses asset valuations. Secondly, non-dollar assets typically face a significant liquidity discount. Non-dollar assets often suffer from insufficient investor understanding, limited trading depth, and unclear exit paths. Therefore, higher yields are needed to compensate investors for liquidity and cognitive costs in order to attract funds. The ultimate result is that the pricing of the same asset may exhibit a systemic discrepancy between the dollar system and non-dollar systems. 2.5 Summary: The US Dollar is Not Only a Unit of Account, but Also the Core Carrier of RWA's Profit Distribution Rights This chapter demonstrates that RWA's dollarization is not simply a market choice, but rather determined by the settlement, pricing, and liquidity structures. Stablecoins lock in settlement currencies, US Treasury bonds define the yield benchmark, and the DeFi system distributes liquidity; these three elements together constitute a dollar-dominated structure. The combination of these three elements means that the US dollar not only participates in pricing but also controls the entire profit distribution process. Therefore, RWA's true power structure lies not on the asset side, but on the currency side: in this system, dollar liquidity determines pricing power and further influences the distribution of global asset returns.

The Structural Conflict Between the RMB Internationalization Path and RWA

3.1 The Existing Path of RMB Internationalization: A Controlled Opening-Up Logic

If we place RWA within a larger framework, we will find a fundamental issue:RMB internationalization itself is not a completely market-driven process, but rather a highly prudent, phased institutional project.Over the past decade, the core path of RMB internationalization has been mainly driven by three main lines: trade settlement, cross-border investment and financing, and offshore financial markets. In 2025–2026, this will further evolve into a structural phase of multi-polar settlement networks + enhanced de-SWIFT dependence + expansion of cross-border digital RMB pilot programs.

According to SWIFT's 2025 cross-border payment data [5], in Q1 2026, the RMB accounted for approximately 3.13% – 3.46% of global payment currencies, a slight increase compared to the same period in 2025, but still significantly lower than the US dollar's 50%+. The key changes in RMB internationalization in 2025–2026 will no longer be reflected in a simple increase in market share, but rather in a three-tiered structural evolution: a tiered settlement network, a regionalized clearing system, and partial embedding of pricing power in energy and commodities. This includes the expansion of the CIPS system, increased RMB settlement in Middle Eastern energy trade, and the introduction of dual-track settlement mechanisms (local currency or RMB) in bilateral trade by some Belt and Road countries. Simultaneously, the offshore RMB (CNH) market is also expanding, but its liquidity remains highly dependent on policy adjustments. This system has a very clear characteristic: RMB internationalization involves expanding its use within a controllable range, rather than completely free flow. It is under this premise that the emergence of the RWA has begun to bring about structural shocks. 3.2 Theoretical Alignment: Why the RWA Appears to be an Accelerator of RMB Internationalization On the surface, there seems to be a high degree of alignment between the RWA and RMB internationalization. First, RWA can directly map domestic assets (such as revenue rights from new energy power plants and supply chain receivables) to the on-chain market, enabling RMB assets to enter the global allocation system in the form of tokenized cash flows. Second, through the on-chain structure, assets can bypass traditional financial intermediaries, achieving more efficient capital matching. Furthermore, if combined with the pilot program of digital RMB (e-CNY) or offshore RMB stablecoins, theoretically, an on-chain asset market denominated in RMB could be built, but its establishment is contingent upon RWA settlement forming a closed-loop liquidity pool within the RMB system. From this perspective, RWA possesses three potential values ​​in the context of RMB internationalization: increased global accessibility of RMB assets (asset side), increased frequency of RMB use in cross-border pricing (pricing side), and the formation of a new asset application layer for offshore RMB (liquidity side). The problem is that this logic is based on the premise that RWA can complete a closed loop within the RMB system. However, the reality is quite the opposite; the current RWA system operates almost entirely within the USD system. It is precisely at this point that the conflict between theory and reality begins to emerge. 3.3 The Unfolding of the Conflict: When the RMB Path Meets the USD-Dominated RWA Structure In actual project implementation, this conflict is not an abstract concept, but rather gradually manifests at every stage of the structural design. Based on experience from multiple projects, this conflict can be summarized into three typical structural misalignments. First, there is the misalignment of the settlement system. Currently, RWA issuance almost inevitably relies on stablecoins such as USDT and USDC, meaning that both fund inflows and outflows are completed in USD. For example, in the Ondo Finance US Treasury RWA structure, both fundraising and profit distribution are completed through USDC, essentially forming a closed-loop US dollar settlement system. However, under the RMB system, the capital account is not fully open, and cross-border capital flows require strict approval. The result is: on one end is a completely free-flowing US dollar stablecoin, and on the other end is a controlled-flowing RMB system. Under this asymmetric structure, if the project insists on using RMB denomination, it will face insufficient liquidity; if it switches to US dollar denomination, it directly abandons RMB pricing power. Secondly, there is a misalignment between cross-border assets and data. RWA requires asset information and cash flow data to be transparent on-chain, but China has strict restrictions on the outbound transfer of financial and corporate data. This leads to a practical problem: not using the blockchain prevents participation in the RWA market, while using it may trigger data export regulations. In many projects, this problem often doesn't surface during the technical phase, but is directly rejected during the compliance review stage. Third, there's the misalignment of the investor structure. RWA's core advantage lies in connecting global capital, but Chinese assets have long been dominated by domestic institutional investors. For example, in some Hong Kong real estate RWA pilot programs (such as green bond tokenization projects), the participation rate of domestic funds is less than 20%, with international institutions and crypto funds becoming the main sources of funding. Introducing overseas investors involves foreign exchange management, investor suitability, anti-money laundering, and verification of the source of funds. Ultimately, this creates a typical contradiction: assets are domestic, funds are overseas, but there's a lack of institutional bridges between the two. This comparison shows that RWA does not simply move assets onto the blockchain, but rather incorporates them into a financial system already unified by the US dollar. 3.4 Hong Kong as a Buffer Layer: Realistic Solutions and Boundaries under Institutional Misalignment Given that the aforementioned conflicts cannot be resolved in the short term, Hong Kong has gradually become a crucial intermediary structure. Its core function can be summarized in one sentence: without changing the mainland financial system, it provides an interface for Chinese assets to access global capital. (1) Two key institutional tools in Hong Kong First, is the stablecoin regulatory framework. Hong Kong is promoting a stablecoin licensing system (HKMA framework), which allows compliant institutions to issue stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies, and has formed an RWA pilot system. Second, is the RWA pilot project. Including the Hong Kong government green bond tokenization project and the real estate income rights tokenization pilot (issued through an SPV structure), it has become the most important offshore testing ground for RWA, and its investors mainly come from the international market. (2) A typical case: Hong Kong green bond RWA. In this type of project, the structure is usually domestic or Chinese-funded assets, issued through Hong Kong SPV, denominated in US dollars or Hong Kong dollars, and targeted at international investors. Its investor structure has obvious characteristics: international institutions dominate, crypto funds participate more, and domestic funds participate less. Even if the assets come from China, the pricing power is still determined by the Hong Kong SPV + US dollar pricing system, which essentially completes the structural separation of assets in China and prices in offshore. (3) Real problems of the offshore SPV path. Another common path is that Chinese-funded enterprises set up SPVs in Singapore or Hong Kong to map domestic assets onto the chain. However, in practice, this path faces several problems: the compliance of domestic asset transfers, the foreign exchange path for profit repatriation, and tax and regulatory uncertainties. The end result is often that while the structure can be designed, it is difficult to maintain long-term stability. 3.5 Summary: RWA may both promote the internationalization of the RMB and accelerate its dollarization. As the analysis in this chapter shows, the impact of RWA on the internationalization of the RMB presents a dual path: on the one hand, it may improve the global liquidity of RMB assets; on the other hand, it may strengthen the dominant position of the US dollar in on-chain asset pricing. Ideally, RWA can improve the global accessibility of RMB assets and expand the use of the RMB in asset pricing. However, under the current structure, RWA is more likely to result in: RMB assets being incorporated into the US dollar pricing system, profits being distributed in US dollars, and pricing power shifting to the offshore market. This creates a deeper contradiction: should we prioritize ensuring monetary sovereignty or improving asset liquidity? Should we maintain financial stability or accelerate financial opening? At the current stage, China's core choice is essentially a structural trade-off: stability first (monetary sovereignty) vs. opening first (asset liquidity). However, this does not mean the problem has disappeared; rather, it means that all future RWA-related paths will involve a continuous institutional rebalancing between the efficiency of financial opening and the security of the monetary system.

Feasible Paths and Structural Solutions Under Conflict

4.1 Three Realistic Paths: Not Multiple Choices, but Optimal Solutions Under Different Constraints

As a basic fact has been established above:The RWA system is dominated by the US dollar, while the RMB system is still in a controlled opening phase.Under this premise, there is no perfect solution in the market; there are three feasible paths, each of which is essentially a trade-off result under different constraints. The first path, and the current mainstream in the market, is the fully dollarized path. Its logic is very straightforward: assets are packaged through offshore SPVs, funds are raised in USD stablecoins, and profits are distributed in USD. The advantages of this path are ample liquidity, a broad investor base, a mature pricing system, and it can almost directly access the existing RWA market. However, the problems are equally obvious: asset pricing power and profit distribution power are completely transferred overseas. The second path, which has been increasingly discussed in recent years, is the offshore RMB RWA system. This model typically relies on Hong Kong or Singapore and is issued and denominated in CNH (offshore RMB). Theoretically, this can retain RMB pricing power to some extent while avoiding direct contact with domestic capital controls. However, the practical challenges are: limited liquidity of offshore RMB, a small investor base, and a lack of mature yield curves. Therefore, this path is more suitable for policy pilots than large-scale commercialization. The third path, which is currently the most valuable for research, is the dual-currency RWA structure. Its core idea is to maintain the RMB system on the asset side and connect to the USD system on the financing side, achieving a bridge between the two through structural design. This means: (1) Underlying assets → RMB denominated (2) Investment funds → USD entry (3) Profit distribution → Dual currency design The difficulty of this model lies in its complex structure, but it is also the path that can currently achieve a balance between sovereignty and liquidity. 4.2 Key Structural Design: Three Core Modules from Energy Development to Sustainability In the actual implementation of RWA projects, choosing the right path is only the first step. The key factor determining whether a project can operate stably in the long term is whether its structural design can balance the triple constraints of regulatory requirements, capital liquidity, and profit distribution. These three not only need to cooperate with each other, but also must be sustainable to ensure that the project can adapt to possible future policy and market changes. (1) SPV Architecture: A Bridging Method Between Domestic Assets and Offshore Operations Currently, the main structural model between domestic assets and offshore operations is to map cross-border assets through SPVs (Special Purpose Vehicles). The specific process is shown in the figure. This structure effectively prevents the substantial outflow of assets, achieving the connection between domestic assets and overseas funds through the transfer of beneficial rights. However, the key optimization point lies in ensuring the compliant repatriation of funds. Many projects fail not because of flawed structural design, but because they cannot effectively explain how funds can compliantly repatriate, especially when it comes to profit distribution. Therefore, it is essential to clearly define the repatriation path of funds and effectively isolate potential legal risks through a multi-layered SPV structure. (2) Profit Distribution Mechanism: Restructuring of RMB and USD In a dual-currency RWA, the design of profits is undoubtedly the most crucial part. Typical profit distribution schemes include: locking in local currency profits + exchange rate hedging, dual-track profits (RMB and USD tiered), and a pre-set exchange rate range distribution mechanism. For example, investors subscribe to USD, but the project's profits are generated in RMB, and the exchange rate difference is locked in through hedging instruments. However, it is important to note that exchange rate fluctuations are not just a technical issue, but also a cost issue. Highly volatile exchange rates can directly increase hedging costs, thereby affecting the overall rate of return. According to calculations, the common cost ranges for exchange rate hedging are as follows: This indicates that if the yield on the underlying asset cannot cover the hedging costs, the attractiveness of the entire RWA structure will be significantly reduced, especially when the yield is low, which may lead to capital outflow from investors. (3) Technical Layer: Introduction of Multi-Currency and On-Chain Financial Instruments With the continuous advancement of technology, the technical implementation solutions of the RWA project have gradually matured, especially in the application of multi-currency stablecoin systems, on-chain lending and liquidity pools, and foreign exchange and interest rate derivatives. These technical tools provide effective support for asset liquidity management and risk hedging: multi-currency stablecoin system (USD + CNH), on-chain lending and liquidity pools, and foreign exchange and interest rate derivatives. For example, in the DeFi (Decentralized Finance) ecosystem, liquidity management can be achieved through stablecoin pools, profit redistribution can be realized through the interest rate market, and some market risks can be hedged through derivatives. These technical means not only reduce the friction existing in traditional finance, but also increase the liquidity and flexibility of assets. However, it must be emphasized that while these technologies can effectively reduce market friction and risk, they cannot eliminate institutional differences. Especially in cross-border projects, challenges at the legal and policy levels, such as regulatory compliance and data privacy, still exist. 4.3 Offshore Issuance Path and Constraint Mechanism of RMB Real Estate RWA If we do not perform abstract modeling but directly look at the structures that have occurred or are progressing in the market, we can find a very typical real-world path: Using domestic real estate cash flow as the underlying asset, offshore tokenization is conducted through Hong Kong or Singapore SPVs, with USD stablecoins as the main fundraising and settlement tool. This type of structure has already begun to take shape in several projects between 2024 and 2026, particularly concentrated in Hong Kong's RWA pilot framework and offshore financing attempts by Chinese institutions. For example, Hong Kong's pilot program in green bond tokenization (related to the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's Project Genesis framework), and some real estate income rights structured financing projects, all exhibit similar structural characteristics. Structurally, these projects typically do not directly issue real estate tokens, but instead employ a three-layer nested structure to achieve compliance isolation: The first layer is the domestic asset layer. The assets are usually commercial real estate or industrial parks in first-tier or strong second-tier cities, held by domestic operating companies, and generating stable rental cash flow. This part remains legally unchanged; the core purpose is to prevent direct cross-border asset transfer. The second layer is the offshore SPV layer. Typically established in Hong Kong or Singapore, the SPV holds the revenue rights or contractual rights to domestic assets, rather than directly holding the assets themselves. The essence of this structure is that legal ownership remains within the country, but cash flow rights do. The third layer is the on-chain issuance layer.
